text: EUGENIEN, a canal of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, extending from the Rhine at Rheinberg, to the Meuse, which it joins at Venlo.
EUGENIUS-VORGEBIRGE, a village of Hungary, in the comitat of Pesth, on the Danube, at the foot of a hill of the same name. It has a fine castle belonging to Prince Eugene of Savoy.
EUGIES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 6 m. S of Mons. Pop. 1,517. It has quarries of freestone.
EUGINO, an island in the gulf of Bothnia, near the coast of Finland, in N lat. 63° 50'. It is the largest of the group which lies to the W of Gamla- Karleby.

EU-HO, or Yun-Liang, a river of China, which takes its rise in the SE part of the prov. of Shen-si; passes through the N part of the prov. of Honan and the S of that of Chih-le; enters the prov. of Shantung, in which it flows in a very sinuous course through a low flat country; and unites with the Cha-ho or Imperial canal, which thence bears the name of this river to its junction with the Pei-ho, in the prov. of Chih-le, in N lat. 39° 11', and long. E of Pekin 0° 48'.

EULALIA, a township in Potter co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 187 m. NNW of Harrisburg, EUP drained by the E branch of the Sinnemahoning river and its tributaries. It has a hilly surface and possesses little cultivation. Pop. in 1840, 368.
EULALIA (SANTA), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Leon, partido of Ponferrada.
EULALIA-DE-LAS-MANZANAS (SANTA), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Leon, partido of Murias-de-Paredes.

EULALIA-DE-MERIDA (SANTA), a village of Mexico, in the prov. and 11 m. N of Chihuahua. The working of an adjacent silver-mine forms the chief employment of the inhabitants. EULALIE (SAINTE), OF SAINTE EULALIE-DELARZAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Cornus, 15 m. E of Saint- Affrique, near the source of the Cernon. Pop. 1,800.
EULALIE - D'ANS (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Haulefort, 18 m. ENE of Perigueux, on the L bank of the Haute-Vezère. Pop. 1,077.

EULBACH, a village of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of Starkenburg, 4 m. from Erbach, at an alt. of 1,650 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 66. It has a hunting-lodge belonging to the counts of Erbach-Erbach.

EULE, a royal town of Bohemia, in the circle of Kaurzim, 14 m. S of Prague, near the confluence of the Tasawa with the Moldau. Pop. 1,350. It was formerly noted for its gold-mines.—Also a town in the circle of Leitmeritz, 7 m. W of Tetschen. Pop. in 1837, 947.

EULE (Hohe), a mountain of Prussia, in Silesia, in the regency of Breslau, 12 m. SW of Reichenbach and 24 m. N of Glatz. It rises to the height of 3,036 ft. above sea-level, and forms the loftiest summit of the Eulengebirge.
EULENBERG, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 18 m. N of Olmütz. Pop. 320. It has a castle, now partly in ruins.

EULENGEBIRGE, a mountain-range in Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, which branches from the Riesengebirge, near Falkenberg, and terminates at the Neisse, presenting an extent of about 24 m. Its loftiest summit is the Eule-Hohe.
EULOI, a village of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. and 39 m. WSW of Trebisond, on the Black sea. Wax and honey are collected in the locality in large quantities for export to Constantinople.

EUME, or UME, a river of Spain, in Galicia, which takes its rise in the Sierra-Mondonnedo, runs W, and, after a course of 36 m., flows into the bay of Betanzos, near the point of the same name, 10 m. ENE of Corunna, in N lat. 43° 27'.
EUNIEH, or Unieh, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. and 120 m. N of Sivas, on the shore of the Black sea.
EUPATORIA. See EVPATORIA.

EUPEN, or NEAUX, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency of Achen. The town is 7 m. SSW of Achen, on the Weege, or Wester. Pop. in 1837, 11,678; in 1846, 10,940. It is a large town; has numerous churches, a school, and a custom-house; and is noted for its manufactures of cloth and kerseymere. It has also extensive paper-mills and tanneries, and several manufactories of nitric acid, soap, and chicory. The circle contains 17,852 inhabitants.
EUPHRASIA, a township of Upper Canada, in the Simcoe district, to the W of Collingwood.

EUPHRATES, or FRAT, a river of W. Asia, one of the most celebrated streams of sacred and classical antiquity, flowing through Russian Armenia, and Turkey in Asia; separating the SE portion of the Turkish pash. of Bagdad, or Irak-Arabi, from the Khuzistan of Persia; and discharging itself, after uniting with the Tigris, into the head of the Persian gulf, after a total course of about 1,800 m. It is | composed of two main branches; the western and northern, called the Kara-su, or 'Black water,' by the Turks,-and the eastern or southern, the Murad, or 'River of desire,' the Euphrates of Xenophon. The Murad is the larger and longer stream of the two. Its source, says Haji-Khalfa, is in the Ala-Dagh, and its stream traverses the plain of Bayazid, and then disappears under ground 4 hours' distance from that city. It then reappears, and receives, near Malazghir [Malazghird], another river of the same name [the Padisshank], and traverses all the district of Turuberan and the S part of Armenia Proper. But Morier says that, instead of rising at or near Bayazid, its source was pointed out to him in the S ridge of Ala, 12 m. S of Diadin, and he adds that he crossed no stream in his way westwards from Bayazid. Morier indeed was not permitted to see Bayazid, or pass by it, though within 4 m. SE of it, at a village which he calls Kerdik, but was compelled to take a circuitous route. In his route from Kerdik to Diadin, or Diyadin, he says he crossed a small rivulet within <math>6\frac{1}{2}</math> hours, or 24 m. of that place: and it has been suggested that this might be the stream that runs through or by Bayazid. But the fact is that Bayazid is in the basin of the Araxes; and the water-shed between that river and the Murad-shai runs to the S and W of Bayazid. In Morier's map the source of the Murad is placed 28 geog. m. SW of Bayazid, and 48 SW of Ararat, in E long. 44° 10', and N lat. 39° 8', Bayazid being in 39° 18' N lat. and 44° 45' E long. In Koch's map [Berlin 1850] the sources of the Murad-shai, on the N flank of the Ala-Dagh, are in 39° 25' N, or nearly on the parallel of Bayazid, and 43 m. W of that city. The infant-stream first pursues a NNE course to Diadin, about 16 m. from its source, where it runs in a deep channel of perpendicular rock, at an alt. of 6,000 ft. above sea-level, presenting, in the month of Sept., a shallow stream 20 ft. wide, and is called the Frat by the natives. At Diadin, it turns NW; and at Utchkilissa, 18 m. NW of Diadin, there is a stone bridge thrown across its stream. From this point it runs to the N of W by Kalassur and Karakilissa; and then bending SSW passes Shamur and Malazghird, where it receives the Padisshank or Saris-su from the S. Its course from this point to its junction with the Sharbuhur, coming from the N, is SW by W. It then turns SW by S, and, at a point 7 m. N by W of Mush, receives the Kara-su, or river of Mush, which descending from the W flanks of the Nimrud range on the W of Lake Van has a NW course of about 40 m. to the l. bank of the E. The river of Mush, called Ak-su by the Turks, the Arsanius of Plutarch, and the Arsenius of Procopius, exactly answers to the description of Xenophon, being a small, pellucid, beautiful stream, with many fine villages on its banks. About 180 m. to the W of this, the Murad unites with the Kara-su at Kebban, on the E skirts of the pash. of Sivas, after passing the town of Palu, the ancient Balisbiga, and receiving several large streams on its r. bank, among which are the Tashtakopri-su, the Jondek-su, and the Latshig-su. The Kara-su, or Northern Frat, rises within 100 m. of the Black sea, at a point 30 m. NE of Elijak, in the Aggia-Dagler-i. e., the 'bitter mountains'a term seemingly corresponding to the Achos-Oros of Dionysius Periegetes, and Eustathius, which signifies 'the mountain of sorrow,' and the same range with the Capotes of Licinius Mutianus, or the Cop- Dagh of the Turks, in the district of Caranitis. At the village of Elijak this stream has a bridge of 6 arches, at a point where it joins another stream. receives, within 4 m. of that place, a branch of equal magnitude from the NE, nearly 60 yds. broad, and crossed by a stone bridge. After receiving four other streams from the Cop-Dagh, it makes a large elbow to the S, and receives the Lycus or Binghulsu, a stream fully equal to itself, 6½ hours below the v. of Mamakhatun. Kinneir indeed denies the existence of such a confluence, and maintains that the Binghul-su is the river of Khanus. He says that all the maps are wrong in making it run W to the Kara-su, instead of E to the Murad; and that this river is the Lycus of the ancients. But in this he has been misled by the term Binghul-su, which he supposes to belong to one river only; whereas it is actually given to not less than three streams: the southern source of the Araxes,-that of the Lycus, -and that of the river of Sharbuhur,-for they all spring from different sides of the same range, which is denominated the Binghul-Dagh, i. e., 'the Mountain of 1,000 springs,' just as each of these is denominated Binghul-su, or 'the River of 1,000 springs.' At the point of confluence with the Lycus is a bridge of 8 arches, and from thence to its junction with the Murad at Kebban-Maden, the Kara-su has its course through a narrow valley, bounded on both sides by lofty mountains and tremendous defiles, passing by Erzingan, Kemahk, and Egin. At the ferry near Kebban, the Kara-su is about 120 yds. wide, and has a deep and rapid stream. The elevation of the point of confluence of the Kara-su and Murad, in N lat. 38° 56', E long. 39° 24', is probably 2,600 ft. above The combined stream of the Kara-su and Murad sea-level. above Kebban, forces a passage through the gorge of the northern Taurus, and is 200 yds. broad, with a great depth of water. Below this it flows in a course prevailingly SSW to Sumeisat; and forms a series of rapids in making its way through the different ranges that seemingly oppose its progress all the way to Syria. After escaping the mountains at Juliopolis, it runs 58 m. to Samosata, the modern Sumeisat, in Commagena the northernmost province of Syria, along the E base of Taurus. From Sumeisat, in lat. 37° 31′ N, E long. 38° 23′, 45 m. below Gergen-Kalehsi, the E. is navigable to the sea; and 14 m. below Rum-Kaleh, which is 51 m. S, 69° W of Sumeisat, it approaches within 801 m. direct distance of Bayas on the Mediterranean. The Tigris steamer ascended the E. to this point, near Bir, in N lat. 37°, E long. 37° 58′, 107 m. NE of Antioch, built upon a chalk formation, at an alt. of 6281 ft. above the line of the Mediterranean, and 1,197 m. from the Persian gulf. We are chiefly indebted to Colonel Chesney's official reports, and Mr. Ainsworth's papers on the geography of this region, for the details which follow. It may be proper to observe that the materials of the two large iron-steamers, Tigris and Euphrates, by which the navigation of these rivers was tested, were, in the first instance, sent up the Mediterranean, and then transported up the Orontes river, into Lake Owja-Denghiz, whence, ascending the Ultra-Kara-su, they were disembarked at a spot called Murad-Pasha, near the v. of Gol- Bashi, i. e., 'Head of the waters.' From this spot to Port-William on the river E., 13 m. below Bir, is a distance of 111 m. across the so-called Syrian desert. The first part of the route lay across a hilly but not unfertile tract, of which the summit-level was 1,723 ft. above sea-level. The second part, from Azzaz to Port-William, is undulating and crosses the valleys by the Koat or Chalus, 1,263 ft. above sea-level, and the Sajur 1,363 ft. above sea-level. These plains are everywhere fertile and cultivated, and abound in populous villages along the whole Running WSW to Ashkela, the ancient Brepus, it | route which passes a little way to the S of Antab, the ancient Antiochea ad Zaurum.—The course of in the memory of the oldest inhabitant. During its the E. from Sumeisat to Bales, or Balis, is nearly S, from Bales to its embouchure, its prevailing direction is SE. The site of the forests of the kings of Syria at Bales, 1,053 m. above Kurna, still abounds in wild boars, and the wilderness around with wolves, jackalls, and foxes. At this place commences the growth of tamarisk, the most frequent and the most abundant plant on the banks of the E., from hence to its embouchure. At El-Hamman, a camel's ford, a distance of 45 m. from Bales -equivalent to what Cyrus made in three days' march, and the point where, in all probability, the 10,000 Greeks and the army of Alexander crossed the E.—there are still the remains of a well-constructed causeway, which leads to the ruins of Sura, a city on the great road of Roman and Palmyrean commerce. From Bales to Rakka is a distance of 70 m., of which 60 m. are occupied by a gypsum formation. The forest of Amran, 12 m. S of Rakka, consists chiefly of poplar - trees. The site of Sophenæ was here sought for on the r. bank of the river, by Colonel Chesney, but only a single isolated colossal monument of alabaster was found. The winding and tortuous E. led hence to a rocky pass of tertiary deposits, covered by a volcanic formation, which shelter the marble walls and antique ruins of Zenobia. A sombre country, with jungle and marsh, with an occasional village of Arabs, or small fort with quadrangular mud walls, extends from hence to the small town of Dair or Deir, 211 m. below Bales. The navigation from Dair to Kerkissia (Cercusium) lies through a level country, chiefly marsh, with districts of tamarisk and some cultivated lands. At 30 m. below Dair, the E. receives the Khabur. To the E, beyond the Khabur, the country still is, as it was in the days of Xenophon, full of wormwood. Wild asses or horses are still met with, and bustards are abundant; but ostriches and roedeer are seldom seen. The Tigris steamer succeeded in ascending the Khabur or Araxes for some miles. The opinion advocated by Colonel Chesney, that this is the river which Trajan, after building his fleet in Sinjar, descended to the E., clears up many difficulties in the contradictory reports furnished to us by the historians of the time, who carry the emperor down the Tigris to Babylon. Julian, who professedly followed Trajan's steps, according to Ammianicus, also embarked at this place and sailed down the E. The country preserves nearly the same characters to the small town of Maïardin, about 31 m. from the castle of Rahba or Rahabah, on or near the site of an ancient city of the same name. Amid the stones of the modern castle occur numerous light bricks, with surfaces covered with vitrified bitumen of a dark green, in the same manner as has been observed at Babylon. The next day's journey brought the expedition under Colonel Chesney to the ruins of a considerable place now called Salahiyat. The walls, a noble gateway, and an extensive castle, are all that remain; but these relics are imposing from their appearance of massive simplicity. The country around is a red stony desert. The ruins are tenanted by the Tartarean wolf; a polyodactyl gecko clambers on the walls, a long-limbed pimelia runs across the desert, but the river flows in a valley nearly 300 ft. below, where there is vegetation and abundant animal life. The day of the expedition's departure from Salahiyat was marked by a fatal phenomenon. A dense cloud of dust, raised by a local wind, came travelling across the desert, accompanied in its onward progress by large nimbi, which poured their incessant waters upon the darkened earth. "This hurricane-which entailed the loss of the Tigris-exceeded what had occurred | prevalence the barometer fell this of an inch, which is a great quantity in a climate where the irregular oscillations are almost null. Under so extraordinary a trial, no effort that skill could invent, or courage put in practice, was spared to save the Tigris; but in endeavouring to make a head against the whirlwind she was overwhelmed, and sunk by a power as resistless as it was little to be expected," in 5 fath. water, after a struggle of about 8 minutes after striking the bank. By this catastrophe 15 seamen and 5 natives perished. "In half-an-hour the sun shone in unblemished brightness, and the desert wind blew calmly, where she and the greater part of her brave crew had been. When Julian was at Ana, Amminianus relates that there occurred a 'terrible event:' whirlwinds which blew down the houses and tents, overthrew the soldiers, and caused many boats to sink. The sites of the Corsoti of Xenophon and of Irzah or Ezra-probably a colony of captive Israelites-were in sight of the locality of this sad catastrophe." From Ezra, the remainder of the expedition directed itself by El-Kayim to Ana, a rockenclosed and picturesque town a distance of 70 m. by the river below Ana. On the right, at the rocky passage of Kavabla, is the small town of Rawah. The river curves suddenly round a rocky point, and a long interminable vista of date-groves, and eight successive islands with habitations peeping here and there from a dense foliage of pomegranates and other shrubby trees, announce the town of Ana or Anah, which occupies a fringe of soil in the river-bank, between a low ridge of rock and the swift-flowing waters of the E., 214 m. below Dair. The castle of Ana, whose ruins occupy one of the islands, was built by the Greeks, destroyed by Julian, reconstructed by the Saracens, and destroyed by the Beduins. The passage of El-Buni, about 6 m. below Ana, is one of considerable difficulty. An irregular ledge of rocks and walls here fill the bed of the river, for a length of from 300 to 400 yds., with a broken current from 5 to 6 ft. deep at the lowest season, or from September to March. The object of these subaqueous walls appears to have been to raise the water at low seasons so as to afford a supply to the wheels used in irrigation. The site of the ancient city is on the l. bank of the river, a little below the present town. The expedition on its way downwards to Hit passed the islanded villages and castles of Haditha and Jibba: the latter, the Jubba of the Romans, was ineffectually summoned to surrender by Julian. Between the island of Haditha and the town of Hit, a distance by river of 70 m., the E. is studded with the remains of embankments, besides rocks and shoals, which greatly impede the navigation; and 7 m. above Haditha a wall crosses the river, leaving only a narrow passage of 30 or 35 ft. wide, and 5 or 6 ft. deep in the lowest season, close in with the r. bank, through which the current rushes at the rate of from 5 to 6 m. per hour. Hit, the ancient Is or Acopolis, so celebrated in all antiquity, 131 m. below Ana, was the next station visited by the expedition. Its never-failing fountains of bitumen are used to cover the gopher-boats of the E. and asphaltic coracles of the Tigris, but are converted to little other use. The trade in salt, obtained by the evaporation of the water of the same fountains, is however considerable. The high minaret of Mesjid - Sandabia stands upon almost the last promontory of rocky land that flanks the E.; and to the E low and level plains, in part inundated, now succeeded to an undulating and more diversified country, and stretched to the confines of the visible horizon. At this place the fort of Feluja announced the shortest line across the head of the * alluvium to Bagdad and the neighbourhood of Nahr- Malcha, or the 'Royal canal,' by which vessels proceed from the E. to the Tigris across a neck of land not exceeding 25 m. in breadth. About 190 m. below Hit is the considerable town of Hillah, at which place the river is crossed by a bridge of boats. Diwanea, the next station to Hilla, is a date-encircled and walled-in v., with extensive gardens. It is approached through a continued country of date-trees, forming groves of exceeding beauty, and a fringe of verdant columns, whose uniformity seldom palls upon the eye. Hence onwards to Lamlum or Lemlum the country is low and less cultivated, and the tamarisk covers whole tracts of land. The navigation of the river through the marshes of Lamlum-the Paludes Babylonia, a tract about 40 m. long, by as many broad, nearly the whole of which is covered with ricecrops as soon as the river recedes-was attended with some difficulty to a large steamer; the bed is here narrow, the windings are numerous and abrupt, and the banks low. The country around the marshes is sometimes below the level of the waters of the E., and the mat huts of the peasants, built upon the inundated rice-grounds, are often in a similar situation. The town of Sukelsheyukh, 137 m. below Lamlum, is the metropolitan city of the Montefigue Arabs, and contained upwards of 10,000 inhabitants before the plague of 1832. It is still the seat of considerable inland commerce, and the place of exportation of the horses reared by the tribe to whom it is subject. The E., from Sukelsheyukh to Kurna, is a noble river; its bed is wide and deep, its waters are clear, and its banks for the most part a wide extent of grassy plain or of reed-marsh, stretching as far as the eye can reach. At Kurna, the confluence of the rivers Tigris and E. takes place. The common channel of the E. and the Tigris from this point to the head of the Persian gulf is known by the name of the Shat-el-Arab or river of Basra. From Kurna to Basra or Bussorah is 42½ m. The conjoined stream of the E. and the Tigris has here an average breadth of 600 yds., with a depth of 21 ft. From Basra to Mohammarah, where the Karun joins the Shat-el- Arab, the distance is 221 m.; and the Shat-el-Arab has a breadth of 700 yds., with a depth of 30 ft., and presents 5 large islands in its channel. The subjoined cut will illustrate the connexion of the Karun, or ancient Eulæus, with the E. The Karun communicated with the sea by a direct and an indirect channel. The direct or natural mouth, and the only one by which it formerly emptied itself into the sea, is the Kor-Bamushir (b), forming also the next mouth of the E. to the east of the Shat-el-Arab (a), with which it runs parallel, at a distance of only 3 m. This ancient mouth of the Karun has still a 4at depth of not less than 9 ft. at low water. The indirect channel is by the hafar or canal (cc), an artificial cut through which the Karun now discharges the greater part of its waters into the Shat-el-Arab. This hafar is about \( \frac{3}{4} \) of a m. in length, from 200 to 400 yds. broad, and has a depth varying from 30 to 42 ft. On either side of the hafar, and half-way between the Shat-el-Arab and the Karun stands the town of Mohammarah. Mr. Ainsworth estimates the hydrographical basin of the E. at 108,000 geog. sq. m., and that of its confluent the Tigris at 36,000. Kurna, one of the three Apameas built by Seleucus, in honour of his first wife Apama, is situated at the point of a triangle formed by the confluence of the two streams. Sir John Malcolm, who was sent as ambassador from India to the court of Persia by the Marquis Wellesley, considering the position of Kurna peculiarly advantageous, and as occupying a point where an impregnable fort might be erected at a trifling expense, which would secure the navigation of both rivers, repress the inroads of the neighbouring Arabs, and command the countries between Bagdad and Basra, recommended this object as worthy of the attention of the governorgeneral of India. Here the channels of the E. and Tigris are so deep, that a small ship-of-war might anchor close to the works; and a canal cut across the base of the triangle, from one river to the other, would render any other fortification unnecessary. Canals.] No subject has more excited the attention of the learned than the great canals and lakes dug for the reception of the waters of the E. at the season of inundation. Of these, the Nahr Isa, which originates from the E. to the N of Felujah, and strikes the Tigris a little to the N of Bagdad, is one of the most important. It inundates for 6 months a large district of country. It is the Barax or Baia Malcha of Ammianus Marcellinus.—The Nahr Sarsar crossed the peninsula between the two rivers a little below the Isa. It is the Maogamalcha of Ammianus.—The Nahr Malikah, Flumen regium, or Royal canal, connected the two rivers at a point still further S.—The canal of Pallacopas, dug by the first of the Babylonian kings, is still in existence. It is cut from the r. bank of the Euphrates, and that part of it which still holds water extends to within 5 m. of the city of Meshid-Ali, or Nejiff. The remainder is nearly choked up with sand, but its course may be traced from the Bahr- Nejiff, or sea of Nejiff, to the town of Zobeir, and to its termination at the Khore-Abdallah in the Persian gulf. In the neighbourhood of Babylon are still the remains of two lakes, celebrated by the names of Ali and his son Hussein. The upper lake lies nearly on the parallel of Babylon. At its N extremity stands the town of Kerbela. From the S extremity of this lake to the N point of the lower, or Bahr-Nejiff, the distance is about 25 m. Into this lower lake the E. was turned by the canal of Pallacopas, at the season of its floods. The opening and shutting of this canal was a part of the office of the satrap of Babylon; and, as Dr. Vincent remarks, it must have been a duty of the highest importance, in a tract of country where all is desert that cannot be watered, and every spot fertile that can be flooded. While Babylon was the capital of the East, the command of the waters of the E. was highly necessary for the cultivation of the adjacent districts; and thus, as the cities in the vicinity of its banks have flourished or decayed, its canals and dykes have been preserved or neglected. Arrian gives us the following account of the origin and object of the canal of Pallacopas, cut from the E., to draw off its superabundant waters. This river, he tells us, descending from the mountains of Armenia, flows within its natural channel during winter, but receives such an accession of water in the beginning of spring, and about the summer-solstice, that, overflowing its banks, it lays under water the plains of Assyria; and unless this inundation, caused by the dissolution of snows in the mountains, were diffused by means of the cut of Pallacopas over lakes and marshes, the whole of the surrounding country would be desolated. The waters thus drawn off reach the main channel again by a diversity of subterraneous courses. Though the E. decreases after the dissolution and discharge of the snows, yet so great a part of its waters flow through the Pallacopas, that had not means been adopted to bank up this channel, and confine the river within its natural limits, the whole E. would probably discharge itself in this direction, and refuse its waters to irrigate and fertilize the plains of Assyria. On this account the satrap of Babylon, at a great expense of labour, cut off the communication; but the work, on being completed, was found to be insufficient; the embankment being composed of mud and such like materials, was incapable of resisting the pressure of the water, and continued to yield it a passage through the canal. Alexander, consulting the advantage of Assyria, shut out the waters by a more solid and permanent work. At a short distance, a rocky soil was found, which, when cut through, and prolonged to the Pallacopas, offered the double advantage of conducting the superabundant waters without injury to its banks, and of affording a good foundation for the constructing of works and sluices to admit the waters of the E. at the season of inundation, and to exclude them effectually when the floods had subsided, and when it became necessary to confine the river within its natural channel, for the cultivation of Assyria. Alexander sailed to Pallacopas, falling down the canal into the Bahr- Nejiff. Here having fixed upon a convenient spot, he founded a city, defended by walls. Thus far we follow Arrian. This city long retained the name of Alexandria, after that of its founder, but on its becoming the residence of a dynasty of Arabian princes, its name was changed to that of Hira. It is now known by the name of Nejiff or Meshid-Ali, and is supplied with water by a subterraneous aqueduct connected with the cut of Pallacopas. This the Wahabis, in order to distress the city, broke down, so that when Captain Kinneir was at Meshid-Ali, in 1808, the inhabitants of the town were obliged to bring their water in sheep-skins, from a distance of three or four miles.—The Bahr-Nejiff, or sea of Nejiff, (the Rahemah of D'Anville) is of equal antiquity with the Pallacopas, and a work of infinite labour. Kinneir passed through the middle of it, in his way from Samavat to Meshid-Ali, and found it dry, with the exception of a few ravines and channels of water, near which the poorer classes rear rice and vegetables.—The only canal at present communicating with the two rivers, below the Lemlun marshes, is called the Hie. It cuts the Jezirch exactly half-way between Bussora and Bagdad, and in spring is navigable for large boats. It runs from a point on the E 14 m. N of Kut, and 90 m. above Kurna, to Kut- Amarah on the Tigris, 241 m. from Kurna. Navigation.] "However much the opinions may vary as to details," says Colonel Chesney in one of his official despatches after having accomplished his survey of the E., "I conclude that every man who has descended the river with his eyes open must consider the E. navigable throughout the year with proper-sized vessels, and also that there is an ample supply of fuel along the banks, of different kinds; the rest, therefore, is a mere matter of detail, to be considered by government, in case of coming to the decision to open the river permanently. I found the state of things much more favourable than I had even dared to expect throughout the whole line from Bira to Basra, with the exception of the Lamlum marshes; there alone the difficulties came up to my previous reports, and they are not by any means insurmountable; indeed I should expect that before many years pass of open navigation, it will be admitted on all hands that the great river, considering its length, is one of the most navigable in the world. The favourable state of our relations with the Arabs encourages me to believe that we may remove our station from Port-William to the neighbourhood of Bales, which is but 45 m. from Aleppo, and actually the nearest point on the river to the Mediterranean; which change would curtail the land-journey about 5 hours, and at once shorten the ascent and descent by 100 m. or a little more, according to the spot selected. From Bales to Kurna the distance is but 1,053 m., of which nearly one-half,—viz., from Bales to Al-uzz,—is a good deal more rapid than the remaining portion from Al-uzz to Kurna. Occasionally the current of the upper river is 4 m. per hour; and even more at times for a short distance; but there was no spot where this vessel did not stem the current every day with triumphant ease, even during the first rush of the sudden freshes which we met with this season during the increase and decrease of the water. We had to deal with the stream in its most rapid state during May and June; throughout the other months it is much more moderate, so much so, that taking the whole river throughout the year, I suspect it will be found that the average is something short of 21 m. per hour; and if I am right in the calculation, a vessel possessing the same speed that this one does might reach Bales in about 140 hours' steaming, deducting of course delays for fuel, and halting some of the nights. With a vessel drawing no more water than the Tigris was intended to do, there would not be any difficulty from rocks, shoals, or any other cause between the Lamlum marshes and Bales throughout the year. "The difficulties caused by the narrow and sharp turnings through the Lamlum marshes, for the distance of about 40 m., may be overcome in three ways. The first is, to ascend the Tigris to Bagdad, and cross from thence by the canal (which is to be cut) to the E. The second, to cut a canal for about 18 m. through the marshes, so as to avoid the windings altogether. And the third is, to construct a vessel of small size and expressly suited to the windings alone. On the first route, it may be observed that the ascent by the Tigris would increase the distance nearly 100 m.; but, with this disadvantage, that line would answer, although it is scarcely necessary to go round by the Tigris when despatches, &c. may be sent from Maedam on the E., which is about 19 m. from Bagdad. As to the second, I consider it one of the easiest tasks possible to unite the open parts of the river above and below the marshes, by cutting 19 m. of a straight canal; but this is rather a matter for future than present consideration, since we may at once follow the third course, by constructing a small steamer for that part of the river alone. She must be short, to turn readily, say about 55 ft. long, by 14 ft. beam: and drawing 21 ft. of water, with two engines, each of 8 horse power; and the paddles protected by wooden or iron fenders. She would accomplish her passage through the marshes in less than one day, and therefore little accommodation would be required for the passengers; indeed she might be quite an open boat. Below the marshes this vessel would answer admirably, taking the distance between Kurna and Lamlum, or the sea and the latter place, which is in all about 269 m.; and it is obvious that the crew of this steamer might take the little one up and down the marshes without having any thing more than a few men to take care of her when not steaming. In this view of the matter, one small vessel, with two rather larger and more powerful than the Tigris was, would be required to open the river for a permanent line of mails with speed, economy, and safety. I entertain a firm belief that a little more intercourse would make all things go on peaceably with the Arabs; but an untoward event might ruin every thing, and after seeing so many of my followers taken away lately in an instant, I ought to be cautious in saying much; yet the chances of warfare and accidents from storm seem to me very small compared with what it is my duty to suppose as a matter of caution; but it is all-important for some years to come to select individuals in every class who are likely to bear and forbear. Kurna seems to be as good a station as could be chosen, and quite as good as Mohammerah; there and at Bales also there should be such establishments as would do a great deal in the way of alterations or repairs. A consular agent, with £200 a-year and leave to trade, might be placed at each, and a third at Ana, having a section of the river to superintend, as to the depôts, &c., and employing natives to collect the fuel at the different points indicated in the tabular view of our descent, or others which may prove to be better suited." Mr. Ainsworth's testimony on this subject is equally decisive. "The river E.," he says, "is eminently a navigable one. I am acquainted with it from Samsat in the Taurus, to its embouchure in the Persian gulf, a distance of upwards of 1,200 m.; and in that extent there are only two real difficulties, both of which are superable by undergoing an expense quite disproportioned to the importance of rendering efficient at all seasons of the year, and throughout so lengthened a course, the navigation of this noble river. The first obstacle is the Karabla rocks at Ana, where there is already a diving-bell at hand, and through which I believe it is the opinion of the naval officers that a steamer might, even at low water, be warped with a loss of not more than 2 hours, in case of her not being able to steam through them. The second is the narrowness of the bed, and the numerous windings which it forms in going through the Lamlum marshes; but these difficulties-which will not bear comparison with what has already been overcome in our own country, for example, in the Forth below Stirling, on the Clyde at low water above Dumbarton-only proved themselves so to the Euphrates steamer, which is of large dimensions, and parallel of that city, extends from the Tigris to the E. The latter mode of navigating the united rivers appears to me fraught with many advantages; the Lamlum marshes are avoided, and the city of Bagdad is brought into closer relations with Basra and with Hindustan, both in point of intercommunication and of commerce. For the efficient navigation of the E., three streamers, besides the sea-steamers, will be requisite: one at a station below the marshes, -another through the marshes themselves, -and one of greater power for the upper part of the river. By taking the road of the Hy or the Tigris, and crossing from thence by a canal to Falluja, one steamer, succeeding the sea-steamer at the junction of the former with the E., would reach the second station, and be succeeded by one more to the upper station. In a commercial point of view, the close communication thus established with so great an emporium of trade as Bagdad is of the very first importance; nor is the connection that would be established between Basra and Bagdad of a trifling character; but there are also on the river between Kurna and Falluja large towns, as Sukalshiyukh and Hilla, and powerful tribes, as the Muntafik Arabs, who have long been actuated by the spirit of commercial enterprise, and whom it would perhaps not be judicious to overlook. . . The national importance of this navigation is of the most comprehensive character. Every one acquainted with the history of the communication of nations-which, as Montesquieu has ably pointed out, is the history of commerce-must be aware that those circumstances which led to the annihilation of the commerce of the East would be once more revolutionized by the opening now proposed to be effected; and that while civilization might be confidently expected to return to its almost primeval seat, it would do so under a very different aspect, and with vastly improved means over the days of Apis and Ophir, or of Cercusum and Callinicum." Kurna to Basra, ... 434 3484 From Hilla to Basra, RESULT: Total from Port-William to Basra, In September 1836, Colonel Chesney's expedition ascended the Tigris 5484 m. from the sea to Bagdad, in 1044 hours. The average breadth of the Tigris was found to be from 150 to 300 yds., and its depth-excluding shallow places-from 11 to 3 faths., with a current running from 11 to 21 m. per hour, at this the very lowest season of the year. "This stream," says Colonel Chesney, "has always been considered, and I think justly, more difficult than the E., being more tortuous, more rapid, and altogether different at the shallow places; the passages through which are at right angles to the banks, winding in that direction from the one to the other, whereas in the E., the deeper passages through the shallow places are nearly in the general line of the stream, and not so numerous by any means." The sandy shoals alluded to are more numerous and greatly more difficult than those of the E. But they proved on the whole much easier for the Euphrates steamer than was anticipated at the lowest season; and a suitable vessel of shallower draught would pass them all in a straight line at all times of the year. "On the whole," adds Colonel Chesney, "the river Tigris is eminently suited for commercial steam navigation; and believing as I do, that its practicability having been demonstrated, I shall yet see it opened by British capital and enterprise, I mean, if possible, to strike a line of levels from the one river to the other, nearly opposite to Bagdad, in order that they may be connected by the canal proposed to be cut by Daoud Pasha, and which is a favourite plan also with his successor the present governor." The Austrian steamer takes 5 days 12 hours from Trieste to Alexandria, and her average passage to Iskenderún would probably be effected in about the same time." Though the E. flows through one of the most productive regions of the earth, yet the pernicious policy of the Turk renders unavailing the bounty of nature, the baneful influence of despotism here converting fertile plains into sterility, and the habitation of wild beasts. On those banks where once flourished the proudest cities of the world, now languish comparatively insignificant towns; where luxury and abundance were universally diffused, a scanty pittance is gathered; and where mighty conquerors have contended for kingdoms, the wandering Arab of the desert now snatches his spoil. EURA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the grand duchy of Finland, gov. and 20 m. NE of Abo.—Also a town 45 m. N of Abo, on a small lake, which discharges itself into the gulf of Bothnia. Morea, which descends from the E side of the Penta- Daktylon chain, and flows ESE to the gulf of Kolokythia, which it enters about 1 m. SW of Kolokyna. Its principal affluents are the Tchelephina on the I., and on the r. the Magala and Tsoka, on an affluent of which Mistra is situated. It is the Eurotas of the ancients.

EURE, a river of France, which takes its rise in EUR the marshy district of the dep. of the Orne, 14 m. ENE of Mortagne; runs E into the dep. of the Eureet-Loir; passes the village of Vert; thence bends N, watering Chartres, Maintenon, and Nogent-le-Roi; and running for about 10 m. along the N confines of the dep.; at Ivry, enters the dep. of the Eure; runs NNW; passes Louviers, and unites with the Seine about 2 m. above Pont-de-l'Arche, after a total course of about 135 m. Its principal affluents are the Vesgre on the r., and on the l. the Blaize, Aure, and Iton. It is navigable to Saint-George's, a distance of about 66 m. Salt, timber, and firewood, form the chief articles of transit on this river. The E. gives its name to two of the deps. of France.

EURE, a department in the NE of France, lying along the S side of the estuary of the Seine, between the parallels of 48° 37' and 49° 28' N, formed out of the ancient provs. of Normandy, the co. of Evreux, and the co. of Maine in Perche. It is bounded on the N by the dep. of Seine-Inferieure; on the E by the deps. of Oise and Seine-et-Oise; on the S by Eure-et-Loir and Orne; and on the W by Calvados. It is of irregular outline, measuring from NE to SW 68 m. in its greatest length, and 64 m. in its greatest breadth. Area 595,789 hectares, of which 350,000 are arable land, 108,000 woods, 11,000 vineyards, and 19,000 meadows. Its surface belongs to the basin of the Seine, and is watered by that river, and by the Arve, the Epte, the Andelle, the Eure, the Risle, the Iton, the Charentonne, and the Calone. The country is generally level; but a few hills are to be seen on the borders of the Seine, the Risle, and the and moist. — The soil consists of vegetable matter mixed with clay, and sometimes marl, resting on a bed of chalk or stone. About 288,000 hect. are a rich loamy soil; 132,000 calcareous; and 32,000 sandy. It is well-cultivated; and the dep., though densely peopled, produces more corn than the pop. requires. The chief kind of corn grown is wheat; rye, oats, and barley, are also raised. Flax is largely cultivated; and from its length, fineness, and colour, is highly esteemed. Some hemp is also produced. The cultivation of the vine is only attempted in a few choice localities. The horses of this dep. are of the pure Norman breed, and have lately been carefully bred and much improved. The breed of cows is also good; and the calves are much in request in the neighbouring deps. The sheep reared on the coast are highly valued by the marketgraziers; and of late years attempts have been made to improve their wool by a mixture of Merinos. Small game is plentiful. Fruit is abundant, especially apples, from which much eider is made. The wine is of moderate quality, and but rarely bears to be kept longer than three years. Mines of iron and coal exist.—This dep. is an active manufacturing district. Cotton, linen, and woollen cloths of every kind are extensively fabricated; and there are establishments for making copper and zinc wares, nails, pins, jewellery, and trinkets; paper and glass are likewise extensively manufactured. Firewood, timber, cattle, honey, wax, and corn, are furnished to the surrounding districts.—The principal ports of the dep. are those of Quillebeuf, Pont-Audemer, Conte-

EURE, or VASILIKO, a river of Greece, in the ville, and Aizier.—The dep. is divided into the 7 arronds. of Evreux, Verneuil, les Andelys, Bernay, Louviers, Pont-Audemer, and Brienne; which are subdivided into 35 cants. and 791 coms. It forms the dio. of the bishop of Evreux. Pop. in 1801, 402,796; in 1821, 416,178; in 1846, 423,247.—In 1840 there existed 752 elementary schools within the dep., and 603 communal schools.

EURE-ET-LOIR, a department of the NW of プログルド・ファブル・ファブル・ファグル・ログル・ファブル・ファブル・ファブル・ログル・ファブル・ファブル・ファブル・ファブル・ファブル・ファブル・ファブル・ファブ France, formed out of parts of the ancient Orleanais, and a portion of the duchy of Maine. It lies between the parallels of 47° 57' and 48° 55' N; and is bounded on the N by the dep. of the Eure; on the E by that of Seine et-Oise; on the S by those of Loiret and Loire-Cher; and on the W by those of Sarthe and Orne. Its length from N to S is about 60 m.; its greatest breadth 55 m. Its superficial area is 595,921 hect. The principal rivers are the Eure, the Loir, and the Huine; the principal affluents of the Eure are the Avre, the Blaise, and the Vorse; of the Loir, the Ozanne and the Connie.- The face of the country is level or slightly undulating, in general, with a few hills. The highest points do not exceed 524 ft. in alt. above sea-level. The soil in the middle and W part of the dep. is the best, consisting chiefly of thick layers of grey, yellow, or black clay, intermixed with sand or calcareous earth. It is on the whole pretty fertile, but in the SW, especially in the arrond. of Nogent, it is sandy and dry; and there are tracts of land amounting to 5,000 hect. too poor for cultivation. About 310,000 hect. are of a rich loamy quality; and 120,000 calcareous soil. Agriculture is better conducted in this dep. than in most of the deps. of France; and the quantity of corn produced so far exceeds the consumption, that nearly 200,000 qrs. are annually exported, chiefly from the Beauce, to Paris. The wheat is remarkably fine. The cows are small, but yield good butter, with which Paris is supplied. Fat oxen and calves are sent to the markets of Poissy and Sceaux. The sheep in 1839 amounted to 788,768 head; some of the flocks are valuable for their wool, having been improved by crosses with Merinos. Wine is not extensively made in this dep., nor is it of the best quality. The supply of apples is abundant; and cider is the common beverage. There are iron-mines and furnaces yielding about 1,200 tons annually. Manufacturing industry is limited; but leather, paper, cotton goods, serges, flannels, and other coarse woollens, hosiery, hats, caps, household linen, beet-root sugar, and some earthenware, are made. The dep. is supplied with fuel from its own woods, which cover about one-twelfth of the whole surface.-The dep. is divided into the 4 arrond. of Chartres, Chateaudon, Dieux, and Nogent-le-Rottrou; which are subdivided into 23 cant. and 435 com. It forms the dio. of the bishop of Chartres. Pop. in 1801, 257,793; in 1821, 264,448; in 1846, 292,337. In 1840 it had 465 elementary schools; and 410 communal schools.

EUROPAA, a district of Russia in Europe, in the grand duchy of Finland, in the S part of the gov. of Viborg. Kivineb and Mola are its chief towns. EUROPE EUROPE, One of the grand divisions into which geographers have divided the surface of the globe. Among these divisions, E. is that in which, while the works of external nature are on the smallest scale, the development of mind is on the greatest. Presenting an extent of surface amounting to little more than a fifth part of that of Asia or America, and a third part of that of Africa, and inferior in climate and native fertility of soil to a great portion of these regions, our continent shows that surface more crowded with men, improved by cultivation, enriched by productive industry and extensive commerce, embellished with mighty cities and splendid works of art, illumined with the reflections of genius, and invested with preponderance in the scale of political importance. In other regions we contemplate, amid the great physical features of the scene, A world of wonders, where creation seems No more the works of Nature, but her dreams. In E. we behold a yet more elevating spectacle,spirit asserting its supremacy over body,-and man, the lord of nature, pursuing most gloriously the high destiny originally assigned him "to replenish the earth, and to subdue it." In other regions it is matter we wonder at; in E. it is mind we admire. Here Humanity has her chosen seat, and from hence have emanated her noblest gifts. Name.] When and from whom E. received its present appellation is not recorded in authentic history. The word occurs in a poem which has been attributed to Homer, who lived about 1,000 years before the Christian era; and it was certainly familiar in the days of Herodotus, 450 B.C. What is said in the mythology of ancient Greece respecting the origin of the name, scarcely belongs to our province to relate; but it seems to have been originally given to a small district on the W side of the Hellespont, and to have been gradually applied afterwards to the entire continent. Limits.] The continental portion of E. is comprised between the parallels of 36° and 71° 12' N; and the meridians of 9° 30' W, and 60° 50' E. E. is bounded on the N by the Northern Icy sea, whose unexplored waters may be considered as stretching to the North pole. The Western or Atlantic ocean, with its numerous large gulfs, laves its whole western shores. And here it has been usual for geographers to draw the boundary-line of this continent between Greenland and Iceland, so as to include the latter island as an appendage of E., but to leave Spitzbergen to America. From the Atlantic, the straits of Gibraltar lead into the Mediterranean, whose waters lave the whole extent of the blooming southern shores of E., and the N coasts of the African continent; but it is a disputed point whether Malta, Gozzo, Comino, Lampedusa, and Limosa, lying between Sicily and Tunis, ought to be regarded as African or European islands. On the E, the boundary-line betwixt E. and Asia takes a circular sweep to the S of Candia, and then runs N through the Ægean sea or Archipelago to the strait of the Dardanelles, giving the islands of Rhodes, Cos, Nicaria, Samos, Scio, Mitylene, and Tenedos to Asia, and leaving those of Scarpanto, Stampalia, Naxos, Scyros, and Lemnos to E. It then passes onwards through the Dardanelles, the sea of Marmara, and the Thracian Bosporus, into the Black sea, through which it runs in a NE direction, and entering the strait of Jenikale, or the Cimmerian Bosporus, passes into the sea of Azof. Thus far the limits of E. on this side are quite natural and subjected to no dispute; but from the N extremity of the sea of Azof to the Icy sea, the boundaries between E. and Asia are not so easily determined. The ancients appear to have drawn a line at random from the sea of Azof to the Caspian sea; and occasionally to have viewed the winding course of the Don as pointing out the natural limits of either continent. Malte Brun considers the courses of the Manytch and the Kuma as indicating the lowest level of the isthmus between the Caspian sea and the sea of Azof, and consequently the most natural boundaries between E. and Asia in this quarter. Hassel, considering those parts of Kasan, Astrakhan, and Siberia, which did not belong to Russia before the time of Ivan Basilowitsch, as still decidedly Asiatic in every distinctive feature, proposes to exclude them from the geography of European Russia. According to that eminent geographer, the European boundary-line ought to commence beyond the sea of Azof, at the peninsula of Gmutarakan. Passing to the S of the territory of the Cossacks of Tschernomorski and the Don, Hassel's line runs along the W boundaries of the Asiatic Russian governments of Saratof, Pensa, Simbirsk, Viatka, and Perm, to the sources of the Petshora, where it joins the Uralian mountains, along the crest of which the boundaryline is supposed to be continued to the N extremity of the chain; whence an imaginary line drawn to the mouth of the Kara, and thence across to Nova Zembla, completes the eastern limits of E. Two other eminent geographers, Hase and Pallas, have endeavoured to fix the limits of Europe on the E. Hase's line follows the course of the Don to the junction of the Moderaditza, and pursues the latter river to its source, whence passing over to the neighbouring source of the Dura, it is conducted to the Wolga. It then proceeds along the W boundaries of Kasan and Siberia to the Ural chain, which it follows to the Icy sea. Pallas considers the Uralian mountains as forming—to the source of the Bielaja—the natural boundaries between the two continents. To bring this chain into connection with the sea of Azof, he makes use of a ridge of mountains running from that sea, through the steppes of Kuban, down to the Sarpo. His dividing line then follows the course of that river to the Volga; and continues along the bank of the Volga to the junction of the Ulostan, where it crosses and runs northwards to the Pseudo- Ural, a large floetz mountain which runs between Urenberg and Samara, and joins the real Ural near the source of the Bielaja. Both these boundarylines are extremely arbitrary, and are attended with the inconvenience of cutting several governments of the Russian empire, so as to give one part of the same government to Europe, and another to Asia. The following general views of the physical connection subsisting between the Asiatic and European continents are interesting: "E. is in many respects only the western prolongation of Asia, where features of the great central chain of mountains similarly break into ramified systems, turned to the Atlantic; while, on the E, they end or border the Pacific. On each coast there are mighty islands, containing the most energetic populations; and on each continent are the two forms or races of mankind, which alone have advanced in mental development, without any common point of departure hitherto philosophically substantiated. Both quarters have volcanic spiracula in the seas beyond them and on the shores, though not in the same degrees of activity; for, while the craters of many on the mainland of Kamtschatka, in the Japanese islands, and on multiplied points in the Chinese and neighbouring seas, are incessantly incandescent, those of E., with exception of the Italian, are dormant or extinct; and vastations within the period of historical cognizance, sufficient to affect profoundly the permanent interests of a resident population. At the bifurcations of the European continuation of the great mountain-chains of central Asia are dislocations of great extent, among which that formed by the great basin of the Euxine, or antique Axinus, is the most remarkable. Its present outlet at the Bosporus, dating probably not much anterior to the Greek heroic age, was clearly a consequence of increased pressure, produced by the waters of the inland seas increasing their weight towards the S in proportion as the N was hove up; and both the Uralian and Sarmatian arms were cut off from their communications with the ocean, but were not to be converted into marshes and deserts until drained-off by a new outlet, and when the sun could act with power in the process of absorption. Then it was that the emphatical expressions of 'the kings of the isles,' and 'isles of the west,' which designate E. in the oldest human records, were correct in the strictest sense; and until the progressive results had been long in operation, man was not able to reach E. in the strength of numbers but only by families or small clans of wanderers, in canoes or rafts, on the northern ice, or at the isthmus of Thrace, before it was rent asunder by a volcanic percussion, and the local deluges of Hellenic mythology took place." Dimensions.] The great irregularity of its form has rendered it difficult either to determine the medial length and breadth of this continent, or to calculate its superficial extent. The greatest extent of E. is measured by a line running from NE to SW between the mouth of the Kara and Cape St. Vincent. This is generally considered as the length of E., and measures about 3,490 m. Measured along a line running ENE and WSW, from the Uralian chain, near Oursk, to Cape St. Vincent in Spain, it is nearly 3,400 m. The greatest breadth of the continent is measured by a line running from N to S between Cape North and Cape Matapan, two places almost under the same meridian, and distant from each other about 2,420 m. Were we to include the islands belonging to E. in these measurements, the breadth of E. would fall to be measured from Cape Jelani in Nova-Zembla, to Cape Motala in the island of Candia. Superficial extent.] The superficial extent of E. has been variously estimated by different geographers. Our want of information respecting the extent of some countries, and the doubts regarding the real boundaries between E. and Asia, noticed above, increase the difficulty of stating precisely the extent of surface belonging to E. Kitchen reckons 3,258,038 sq. m.; Hassel 3,331,579; and Crome 3,796,349. Perhaps, notwithstanding its irregularity of form, we may ascertain the superficial area of this continent with tolerable accuracy by reducing it to an oblong square. If for angles of this square be taken the North cape in Norway, and the town of Iekaterinburg in the Russian gov. of Perm, Bordeaux in France, and Cape Solomon, the E extremity of the island of Crete, the sides of this parallelogram will measure 2,100 m. and 1,500 m. respectively; and will enclose a superficies of 3,150,000 sq. m.; and the different portions of land excluded from this measurement will amount to about 300,000 sq. m.; making in all a superficial extent of 3,450,000 sq. m. E. thus embraces about one-sixteenth part of the whole terrestrial surface of the globe; and, with all its islands, does not exceed in superficial extent the Australian continent. Divisions. There are in E. no great natural divithough the Azorean cluster turmoils on a similar | sions, unless we consider it as divided by the Baltic scale, Hecla, in the high N, alone has produced de- linto the countries lying N and S of that sea. On the N of the Baltic are the northern or Scandinavian kingdoms, Denmark, Norway, and Sweden. The extensive empire of Russia occupies the remainder of the N and all the E of Europe. In the S is the kingdom of Greece, the European part of the Turkish empire, and the Italian states. In the W, proceeding from S to N, are the kingdoms of Spain and Portugal, France, Holland, Belgium, and part of Germany. And in the centre of E. are situated most of the German states, Prussia, Poland, Switzerland, and the states of Austria. Seas.] E. is particularly distinguished by the seas and numerous gulfs that wash its shores, and the number and extent of its inland waters. Hence the facility with which the commercial intercourse of nations is here conducted; and hence too the influence which Britain has been enabled to exert over the political destinies of E., as a great maritime power. "Nothing," says Professor Guyot, "characterises E. better than the variety of its indentations, of its peninsulas, of its islands. Suppose, for a moment, that beautiful Italy, Greece with its entire Archipelago, were added to the central mass of the continent, and augmented Germany or Russia by the number of square miles they contain; this change of form would not give us another Germany, but we should have an Italy and a Greece the less. Unite with the body of E. all its islands and peninsulas into one compact mass, and instead of this continent, so rich in various elements, you will have a New Holland, with all its uniformity." Upon a comparison of the whole continent, it is found that the coast-line of E., from the straits of Waigatz in the Polar ocean, to the sea of Azof, is 17,200 m.; while the maritime coast of Asia is 30,800 m., Africa 16,000 m., and both Americas 31,000 m. The ratio of the linear miles of coast to the sq. ms. of surface is: for Africa, 1 to 530; Asia, 1 to 459; America, 1 to 359; and for E., 1 to only 164.—Along its N limits this continent has the Arctic ocean, or that portion of the waters of the great Atlantic ocean which lies within the Arctic circle. These waters are probably destined to remain for ever comparatively unexplored. The White sea is a great basin of this sea, into which thirteen large rivers empty themselves. It has three secondary gulfs: viz., the Droinskaja, Onegskaja, and Kandalaskaja; and freezes every year.-Doubling Cape Stat, the W point of Norway, we enter that part of the great Western ocean called by navigators the German ocean, and now more generally the North sea. This sea extends from the Shetland isles to the straits of Dover, along the E shores of Great Britain, and washes the W coasts of Belgium, Holland, Germany, Denmark, and Norway. It has made many remarkable encroachments upon its coasts, and an immense deposit of debris is spread over a great proportion of its bottom, while in some places, as at the mouth of the Elbe, it has admitted of the enlargement of the land by alluvial deposits. The submarine plateau of this sea nowhere sinks lower than 600 ft., and between France and England its greatest depth does not exceed 300 ft. But at the edge of the plateau, to the SW of Ireland, it suddenly sinks 2,000 ft., and we may therefore say that here the basin of the Atlantic really begins. [Guyot.] -The English and the Dutch have given the name of the Sleeve to that narrow part of the German sea which divides Norway from Jutland, sometimes called the Skager-Rack: Malte Brun suggests the Norway or Jutland channel as a more appropriate designation. This channel, turning S near Cape Skager, forms another and a narrower channel, crowded with islands and rocks, called the Cattegat; and from the latter channel three straits, viz., the Sound, and the Great and Little Belts, conduct into the Baltic.—The EUROPE. Baltic, named the Eastern sea by the Scandinavians and Germans, washes the inner shores of Germany, Prussia, Russia, and Sweden, and has two large arms, the gulfs of Bothnia and Finland. The gulf of Bothnia being almost divided from the main waters of this sea by a line of islands extending from Finland to Sweden, has been compared to an independent lake; and the gulf of Finland, into which numerous rivers discharge themselves, and whose waters are consequently fresher than any other portion of the Baltic, and are affected by the periodical swellings of its tributary streams, has been, with still greater propriety, compared to the embouchure of a large river. The land-locked basin of the Baltic is frequently agitated by dreadful storms; and the dangers of the navigator are here increased by the shoally coasts of Prussia, and the numerous strands and cliffs of the Swedish shore. During the melting of the snow, in summer, a strong current runs into the North sea from the Baltic, by the Sound and the two Belts; at other seasons, the currents are regulated by the prevailing winds. Between the coasts of Germany and Sweden the Baltic has a depth of only 120 ft.; further N, it becomes deeper. This inland sea is usually frozen four months annually; but the rigour of the climate appears to have considerably diminished, as the duration and extent of the ice was much more considerable here in the 14th and 15th centuries than now. The tides of the Baltic are scarcely perceptible, and its waters are comparatively fresh.—The straits of Dover, or the Pas de Calais, connect the North sea, on the S, with the English channel, or La Manche, an arm of the Atlantic extending between England and France, and subject to high and impetuous tides.—The Mediterranean stands connected with the Atlantic ocean by the straits of Gibraltar. It is a deep sea, particularly in its W basin. On the S of Spain, its depth exceeds in some places 6,000 ft.; and on the NW of Sardinia a depth of 5,000 ft. has been measured without touching bottom. The straits of Gibraltar, the most important in E., are about 28 m. in length, and measure about 8 m. across at their narrowest point. In the middle of the straits a strong current sets in from the Atlantic, while two feebler lateral currents, flowing in the opposite direction, convey the waters of the Mediterranean into the Atlantic. The Mediterranean has two extensive arms, the Adriatic sea and Archipelago, which still further enhance the eminent value of this sea as a highway of communication between distant regions of E. Its surface is sprinkled with a vast number of beautiful islands, some of which are extensive, and were formerly the abodes of civilization and refinement; and its coasts have always been the nursery and theatre of ancient and modern civilization.—The Black sea enters from the Mediterranean by the straits of the Dardanelles, the Propontis or sea of Marmara, and the Bosporus or narrow channel of Constantinople. The shores of this ocean are low. Russian maps assign to it a depth of above 3,000 ft. S of the Crimea. It waters are not so pure as those of the Mediterranean; they are likewise fresher,—a quality they probably owe to the many large rivers which discharge themselves into this inland sea. The Black sea is connected by the straits of Caffa, or Cimmerian Bosporus, with the turbid waters of the Palus Maotis, or sea of Azof. Some geographers are of opinion that the latter sea communicates with the Caspian by means of subterranean channels. Malte Brun considers it not improbable that a strait formerly connected these two seas, and that it has been gradually obstructed, in the course of ages, by gravel and alluvial deposits from the torrents of Caucasus. Islands.] The European islands are numerous, and some of them extensive and highly important. On the W, in the Atlantic ocean, are the Azores, situated about 13° W of Cape St. Vincent in Portugal. Though frequently classed under Africa, they more properly belong to E., to which they are nearer, from which they are peopled, and to which they have always been subject.-The British isles, consisting of Great Britain and Ireland, with the small circumjacent islands, are the seat of the greatest and most powerful empire on the face of the earth.—The Faroe islands belong to the crown of Denmark. The large and celebrated island of Iceland, subject to the same power, lies about 400 m. from the continent, in the Western ocean; and numerous other unimportant islands are scattered along the Norwegian coast .- In the great Northern sea, the chief and most extensive are the remote and dreary and almost uninhabited islands of Nova-Zembla and Spitzbergen .-- In the Baltic are the islands of Denmark, Sieland, the chief seat of the Danish monarchy, Funen, Laaland, Falster, and Bornholm; the islands of Oeland and Gothland, subject to Sweden; and of Aland, Cronstadt, Oesal, and Dago, subject to Russia.—In the Mediterranean are the islands of Majorca and Minorca, Corsica and Sardinia, the large and fertile island of Sicily, Malta, Candia the ancient Crete, Negropont the ancient Eubæa, with the numerous other islands of the Grecian archipelago. Peninsulas.] Europe contains numerous peninsulas: some of its finest and most celebrated regions are peninsular. Besides innumerable others of lesser extent and importance, we may notice Crim-Tartary in the Black sea; the peninsulas of Greece and of Italy in the Mediterranean; Spain and Portugal, contained between the Mediterranean and the Atlantic; and Jutland, stretching between the Atlantic and the Baltic. The whole of Scandinavia is a large peninsula formed by the Baltic and the Northern ocean. Lakes. E. has numerous inland lakes. The greater part of these are situated in Switzerland, Upper Italy, Germany, Hungary, Russia, Scotland, and Sweden; but few of them can be compared, in point of extent, to those found in other parts of the world. The largest European lake is the Ladoga, in Russia, which has a superficial extent of 6,340 sq. m.; the Onega, in the same country, is about half that size, and greatly exceeds the largest of the remaining lakes. The Saimas lake in Russian Finland has an area of 1,602 sq. m.; the Wener lake in Sweden has an area of 2,135 sq. m.; the Wetter lake, 840 sq. m.; and the Mœlarn, 760. The lakes of Geneva, Contheir dimensions than any of the preceding. The accumulation of lakes, or insulated pools of water, in some places of E., is very remarkable. Some Russian governments possess an incredible number. the Baltic, along the coasts of the Adriatic, and in several parts of the Mediterranean. Rivers.] E. is abundantly watered, though its rivers are greatly inferior in size to those of the other continents. In fact, our continent is too much intersected by the sea, and presents too small a mass of land, to abound in such magnificent streams as are found elsewhere. In the E part only, where it stands connected with the great Asiatic mass of land, is found any considerable breadth of contiguous territory; and here also do we find its largest rivers. The Caspian receives the Ural, which separates E. from Asia; the Volga, which traverses the greater portion of European Russia; the Kuma, which, according to the system proposed by Malte Brun, EUROPE. should form part of the boundary-line between E. and Asia; and the Terek. All these rivers are Russian.—The Don falls into the sea of Azof; the Dnieper, Dniester, and Danube, enter the Black sea. The course of the last-named river is only inferior to that of the Volga, traversing the whole of S. Germany, Hungary, and European Turkey .- The Maritza and the Vardar, rivers of European Turkey, fall into the Archipelago; and the Po and the Adige flow into the Adriatic. The Tiber, which in its limited course bathes only an inconsiderable extent of the Tuscan territory, and a portion of the Papal states; the Rhone, which flows through the SW portion of Switzerland, and the SE of France; and the Ebro in Spain, all fall into the western branches of the Mediterranean.—The Atlantic ocean and its branches receive the Guadalquiver, Guadiana, Tagus, and Duero or Douro, all of which flow through Spain, although the three last-named terminate on the coasts of Portugal; the Garonne, Loire, and Seine, rivers of France, the first two of which fall into the Atlantic, and the last into the English channel; the Scheldt, the Meuse or Maese, and the Rhine, the Weser, and the Elbe, all falling into the North sea, the first three after having traversed part of France, the Netherlands, and Germany, and the remaining two after watering a great part of N. Germany; the Glommen, the largest river of Norway, and the Göta of Sweden, the first of which falls into the Skager Rack, and the latter into the Cattegat; and the Thames and Humber in England, which fall into the North sea.—The Baltic and its branches receive the Dala, the Indals, the Angermann, the Umea, and the Lulea, from the Norwegiano-Swedish monarchy, and the Tornea from the latter country and the Russian territory; the Neva, the Duna or southern Dwina, and the Niemen, from Russia; the Vistula, the course of which is divided between the Austrian empire of Poland, the republic of Cracow, and of Prussia; and the Oder, the course of which is confined almost throughout to Prussia.—The Arctic Frozen ocean receives the Tana from Finmark in Sweden; the Petshora from the Russian gov. of Archangel; and the Kara-Baigarama, which in part separates E. from Asia.—The White sea receives the Onega, the Northern Dwina or Dvina, and the Mezen, which flow through a large portion of Northern Russia.—Malte Brun calculates that if all the rivers in E. be taken as 1.000,— Those which flow into the Black sea, are 0:273 Into the Mediterranean, including the Archipelago 0.144 and the Adriatic, .
