From the Dwina, 0.031 The largest and most important of the European rivers is the Volga,—the greater part of which belongs to this division of the globe. This river takes its rise in the mountains of Valday, between Petersburg and Moscow, and, after running generally in a SE direction, a course of upwards of 1,700 m., falls into the Caspian at Astrakhan. This noble river, having no cataracts, and few shoals, is navigable even as high as Tver. Its chief tributary streams are the Kama on the E, and the Oka on the W .- Next to the Volga is the Danube, whose magnificent stream rises in Suabia, and passing through Bavaria, Austria, Hungary, and Turkey, falls into the Black sea, after a circuit of 1,300 m. The Danube. though $ occasionally impeded by small falls and whirlpools, is navigable through an immense extent of its course. -The Dnieper, or ancient Borysthenes, rises in the gov. of Smolensk, about 150 m. to the S of the source of the Volga, and after a course of about 1,000 m. through fertile provs., falls into the Euxine.—The Rhine rises in the mountains of Switzerland, and falls into the German sea, by several mouths, on the coast of Holland. This noble river, whose banks are celebrated for their grand and striking scenery, forms the great barrier between France and Germany, and its course may be computed at 600 m. It receives, near Mentz, the tributary stream of the Mayne.—The Elbe rises in the Sudetic mountains of Silesia, and, running through the N of Germany a course of 500 m., discharges itself into the sea near Cuxhaven.—The Don rises from a lake in the government of Tulan, and falls into the sea of Azof, after a course of about 800 m.—The Dniester, forming the boundary between European Turkey and Russia, derives its source from the N side of the Carpathian mountains, and falls into the Euxine at Akerman, after a course of about 600 m.-The Duina and Petshora direct their course to the Arctic ocean. The course of the former, which falls into the White sea, is about 500 m.; and that of the latter 450.—The White Dwina rises in the province of Smolensk in Russia, and falls into the Baltic at Riga, after running 500 m .- The Vistula rises in the Carpathian mountains, passes Warsaw, and joins the sea near Dantzig, after a course of 450 m.-The Oder has its source in the mountains of Moravia, and, after watering Silesia, Brandenburg, and Pomerania, joins the Baltic, after a course of 380 m.- The Rhone springs from the glacier of Furca, and, flowing through the lake of Geneva, bends its course to the S, and enters the Mediterranean. Its course is 400 m.—The Loire rises in Languedoc, and after running 500 m., enters the ocean beyond Nantes .- The beautiful stream of the Seine, on which the city of Paris is built, falls into the English channel at Havre-de-Grace, after a course of 250 m.—The Ebro rises in the mountains of Asturias in Spain, and after a SE course of 350 m., falls into the Mediterranean.—The Tagus has its source in the W of Aragon, and holding a course of 450 m., falls into the Atlantic at Lisbon.-Nor in enumerating the rivers of Europe, ought we to forget the Thames, which, though one of the smallest, is one of the most celebrated; the seat of British empire, and the grand resort of the commerce of the world. Plains. The greater part of E. presents a mountainous surface; but the masses which tour up in the S greatly exceed those of the N. The plains of E. are much inferior in dimensions to the same physical feature in the other continents. With the exception of the wide valley of the Theiss, and the basin of the Po, we do not recognise any extensive plain on the S of the Sudetic chain; but an enormous plain, interspersed only with a few detached ranges of hills, extends from the mouth of the Rhine, over the whole of N Germany and the greater part of Poland, to the foot of the Uralian chain. It has been supposed that this plain at one time formed part of the bed of a continuous sea, of which the Baltic is the only remaining portion. The difference in general elevation between N. and S. Europe may be illustrated by stating, that if the waters of the Atlantic ocean were to rise 1,500 or 1,600 ft. above their present level, the whole of N. Europe, with the exception of the mountainous districts of Norway and Scotland, would be laid under water; while S. Europe, on the contrary, being higher than the level of such an inundation, would form one or two large and high islands. We may form a tolerably accurate idea of the levels III. # EUROPE. of the ancient continent, by tracing a line across it in such a direction as to pass no river; which will obviously indicate a tract of country higher than most of the neighbouring parts. Beginning at Cape Finisterre, we soon arrive at the Pyrenees, keeping to the south of the Garonne and the Loire. After taking a long turn northwards to avoid the Rhine, we come to Switzerland; and may approach very near to the Mediterranean, in the state of Genoa, taking care not to cross the branches of the Po. We make a circuit in Switzerland, and pass between the sources of the Danube and of the branches of the Rhine, in Suabia. Crossing Franconia, we leave Bohemia to the N, in order to avoid the Elbe; and coming near to the borders of Austria, follow those of Hungary to the south of the Vistula. The Dnieper then obliges us to go northwards through Lithuania, leaving the Don wholly to the r.; and the Volga, to pass still farther N, between Petersburg and Moscow, a little above Bjelosero. We may then go E to the boundary of Asia, and thence N to Nova Zembla. Hence we descend to the W of the Oby, and then to the E of the branches of the Volga, and the other inland rivers flowing into the Lake Aral, and the Caspian sea. Here we are situated on the widely extended elevation of India, in the neighbourhood of the sources of the Indus; and, lastly, in our way from hence towards Kamtschatka, we leave the Jenesei and Lena on the l., and the Ganges, the Kian- Kew, the Hoangho, and the Amur on the r. Mountains.] The most elevated districts in E. are Switzerland and Savoy. In the comparatively level regions extending from Iceland to the Caspian sea, the mountains rise in insulated groups; while in the southern and central parts of this continent, or from Etna in Sicily, to the Blocksberg of the Harz, and from the straits of Gibraltar to the Bosporus, all the mountains belong to one great connected system.— In this quarter are the Alps,—the highest and beyond comparison the most extensive range of mountains in E., though scarcely exceeding one-half of the average height of the great S. American chain under the equator. They comprise the great central table-land of E., and, with their ramifications, spread over a sixth part of its extent. Perpetual ice commences here at the elevation of 7,000 or 8,000 ft. At the height of 10,800 ft. the ice disappears, and the atmospheric vapour, congealed as it descends, covers the ground with eternal snow. They branch out, in various angular directions, into the Maritime, Cottian, Graian, Pennine, Lepontine, Swiss, Rhetian, Norian, Carnian, Julian, and Dinarian Alps, which again spread out in many secondary chains.—The Apennines, stretching in a vast crescent through the whole length of the Italian peninsula, and evidently connected with the mountains of Sicily, may be regarded as a southern branch of the Alpine series. The average height of this chain is about 5,000 ft.— A second great chain, known as the Hesperic or Pyrenean system, stretches its branches over the whole peninsula of the Pyrenees. The two outer bulwarks of this peninsula—which consists of a central plain elevated from 2,000 to 4,000 ft.—are the mountains commonly called the Pyrenees, stretching between France and Spain on the N, and the Alpujarras or Sierra Nevada of Spain on the S. The mountains of Auvergne, which are connected with those of Vivarais and the Cevennes, are united to the Pyrenees by the Logere.—At the other extremity of E., three chains of mountains meet together, collectively called Argentaro, at a point nearly equidistant from the Danube, the Adriatic and the Ægean sea. This central point may be considered as the nucleus of all the mountains in European Turkey. From it proceeds the ancient Hæmus or modern Balkan, eastwards to the Black sea. A second range runs NW till it joins the Carnian Alps; and a third runs S through the peninsula, dividing the northern continent of Greece into two parts of nearly equal breadth, and passing into the islands of the Archipelago. As the Hellenic mountains, with the exception perhaps of Athos and Olympus, have never been accurately measured, it is impossible to determine whether or not they are higher than the Apennines. Mount Orbelus, the northern boundary of the country, has, according to Fouqueville, its summit perpetually covered with snow, and must therefore, according to the laws that fix the lower limit of congelation, exceed 8,500 ft. of elevation. None of the other Hellenic mountains, however, whether they be insulated or disposed in groups and ranges, attain the line of perpetual snow. The Carpathian and Hercynian mountains are separated from the Alps and the Hellenic mountains by the Danube. This wild range, the general elevation of which is from 4,000 to 5,000 ft., completely encircles Transylvania, and is connected with the Sudetes or Giant-mountains of Silesia, and the Erzgebirge or Metallic mountains of Saxony.—The mountains of Russia, forming the Sarmatian system, have no visible connection with the other European mountains. The woody heights of Valdai and Duderhof seem to form their centre, from which a number of secondary branches diverge, like radii, into the surrounding country. The British, Irish, and Icelandic mountains are all of secondary rank. Volcanoes.] There are only three volcanoes properly so called, in Europe; viz. Ætna, Vesuvius, and Hecla. Besides these, however, there are nine jokuls in Iceland, and two mountains upon Stromboli and Milo, which emit flames. Some naturalists assert that there are two great subterranean fires under E., one of which cuts the first meridian of the Arctic polar circle, and the other extends under Italy and the Mediterranean, towards the Archipelago. Climate.] The greater part of Europe is situated within the N Temperate zone; about a twelfth part only of its superficies extends within the Arctic polar circle. E. enjoys a higher temp. than any of the other great divisions of the world in corresponding latitudes. If between the parallels of 58° and 38° N, we compare Nain on the coast of Labrador with Gottenburg, Halifax with Bordeaux, New York with Naples, and Saint Augustus in Florida with Cairo, we find the differences of mean annual temp. under equal latitudes in Eastern America and Western Europe, commencing with the most northern pair of stations, and proceeding S, successively 20° 7′, 13° 8′, 6° 8′, and almost 0°. Thus, says Humboldt, "Ireland, Guernsey, and Jersey, the peninsula of Brittany, the Ireland, in lat. 54° 56', under the same parallel as Königsberg, the myrtle flourishes as luxuriantly as in Portugal. The mean mean annual temp.), 49° 2', it is barely 60° 8': the mean winter temps, of the two stations being 27° 7' at Buda, and 39° 8' at Dublin. The winter temp. of Dublin is 3° 6' higher than that of Milan, Pavia, Padua, and of the whole of Lombardy, although they enjoy, on the mean of the whole year, a temp. of at least 54° 8'. Stromness, in the Orkneys, not a 1° S of Stockholm, has a winter temp. of 39° 2', being nearly as mild as London, and milder than Paris. Even in the Färoe islands, in lat. 62°, under the favouring influences of the sea and of W winds, the inland waters never freeze. On the lovely coast of Devonshire, where Salcombe-bay has been called, on account of its mild climate, the Montpelier of the North, the Agave Mexicana has been seen in blossom in the open air, and orange-trees trained against espaliers, and only slightly protected by mats, have borne fruit. There, and at Penzance and Gosport, as well as at Cherburg in Normandy, the mean winter temp. is above 41° 8', that is, only EUROPE. 2° 4' lower than that of Montpelier and Florence. Hence we perceive in what a variety of ways the same mean annual temp. may be distributed in the different seasons of the year, and the important influence of this distribution, whether considered in reference to vegetation, to agriculture, to the ripening of fruits, or to the comfort and well-being of man." Although the astronomical climates are greatly modified in this part of the world by physical causes, we may, in taking a general view of the climate of E., consider it divided into four different districts, the boundaries of which are defined by parallels of latitude.—The Arctic district begins at the 65th parallel, and runs up to the highest north. On the N skirts of this region, nature cannot nourish a tree, nor the industry of man produce a field of corn; all vegetation dies away, except that of the rein-deer moss, destined for the support of the only animal which can bear the rigour of this dreary and desolate clime. Yet it is to be observed that certain species of grain, particularly barley and oats, are cultivated in Norway at the 70°, whilst, on the opposite coast of America, such cultivation ceases at the 52°. The fir, or Pinus abies, is found in the whole of E. within the 67th parallel; and the wild pine reaches to the 68th.—The cold district falls between the 65th and 55th parallels. This region is much milder than the preceding one; but the eastern part of it, which is exposed to the winds which sweep over the high and cold plains of Asia, is much colder than the western part. Thus, the climate of Christiania in Norway, which is defended from the eastern winds by a rocky barrier, is much milder than that of Petersburg under the same parallel. On the other hand, the vicinity of the Atlantic ocean renders the western climate more humid, and the conflicts between the maritime and continental winds occasion sudden and extreme variations in the state of the atmosphere.—The temperate district comprehends the region lying between the 55th and 45th parallels. Here the climate is mild and alike genial to animal and vegetable life. This is the native country of corn and wine in E. It has been observed here that the cold in the northern districts has considerably increased within the last three or four centuries,—a circumstance which some ascribe to the influence of the advancing masses of ice in the Polar regions, and others to the destruction of the forests in France and England. A great part of central E., on the N and W of the Alps, descends by a continued inclination towards the Baltic, the North sea, and the Atlantic; and it is found that the countries on the lowest level of the northern boundary of this inclined plain, possess a temperature little inferior to the more southern and more elevated districts. Thus Normandy is not much colder than Burgundy, and the winter in Denmark is not much longer than the same season in Bohemia.—The warm district extends over the countries of E. lying below the 45th parallel. In the peninsulas and islands of the S of E., the most beautiful vegetation under the most delicious climate occurs. Here an almost perpetual spring reigns; the sugar-cane attains tropical luxuriance in Sicily and Granada; the orange perfumes the air; the streams meander between banks clothed with rose-laurels and myrtles; the acanthus blooms on the ruins of departed greatness; the cypress overshadows the public cemeteries; and the vine and the olive constitute the wealth of the husbandman.—Following the division now proposed, the Arctic district of E. extends over a superficies of about 400,000 sq. m.; the warm district may be estimated at 540,000 sq. m.; while upwards of 1,500,000 sq. m. belong to the cold, and 970,000 sq. m. to the temperate district.— The following table from Malte Brun exhibits the mean annual temp. of various districts in Europe, according to the Centigrade thermometer: Copenhagen. According to Cuvier the total number of mammalia found in E. is 150, of which only 58 are peculiar to this continent. Some species of wild animals have entirely disappeared before the increase of population, while others have become extremely rare. Thus, according to Aristotle, the lion was once found in Greece, but it no longer exists in Europe. The buffalo, the elk, the stein-buck, and the beaver are nearly extinct in E.; and the varieties of game-which once seemed to possess in many quarters, under the protection of merciless tyrants, a fuller right in the soil than man himself—are now confined to juster limits. The rein-deer and the dog are the only domestic animals of the Polar zone. The walrus, white bear, and blue fox, appear on the shores of the Frozen sea. The horse is found to the 66th parallel, but in the high latitudes is reduced to a dwarfish stature; cattle too lose their horns in the northern regions, and shrink in size; even man himself appears here an inferior species, whether physically or morally considered. Some Asiatic animals are found in the neighbourhood of the Caspian and the sea of Azof. The black or brown bear, the urus, and the wolf, are the most formidable wild animals now known in E.; and the lynx and wild-cat attain considerable dimensions in the Italian forests. The great majority of the known species of mice and rats, the hamsters, two species of martins, numerous weasels, one species of badger, an otter, three kinds of dormouse, a species of flying squirrel, several marmots, and the blind and common mole, are exclusively European. Five different kinds of hares and rabbits-one of them apparently confined to Ireland—and a species of beaver, thought to be distinct from the American beaver, may also be mentioned as European.—The total number of birds found in Europe may be estimated at nearly 400, but not much exceeding the half of that amount can be considered as exclusively European. There are four species of European vultures, including the bearded griffin, or lammergeyer. The golden eagle, the Grecian eagle, the spotted eagle, the social eagle, and the little eagle, are commonly accounted European species. Among the most characteristic of the owl tribe belonging to E., are the snowy owl, the great horned owl, the barn owl, and the tawny howlet. The king-fishers are here represented by the common king-fisher, one of the most brilliantly-tinted of birds. Of the true grouse, the largest is the capercailzie; and the most remarkable, on account of its restricted geographical distribution, is the red grouse, which does not exist anywhere but in the British islands; it is, moreover, the only bird peculiar to Great Britain.—E., though not wholly free of dangerous reptiles, suffers less from their presence than any other region of the globe. The mosquito is troublesome in the highest North; and flights of locusts occasionally arrive in Taurida from the African or Syrian coast. Snakes and vipers are found in various quarters; but none of these reptiles are here armed with the deadly virulence of their tribe in other regions of the globe. Vegetable kingdom.] E. is indebted for its most valuable plants to other climes. Originally it probably possessed little more than forest-trees, a few shrubs, and some species of grass. The cereal and leguminous plants are cultivated throughout 5-6ths of E. The olive ceases at the parallel of 44°; but rice is cultivated to about 47°. The vine is successfully cultivated at Witzenhausen under 51° 21' 30", and at Zullichan under 51° 58'; but, with these exceptions, wine manufactured above the 50th parallel does not deserve the name. The region of the vine comprises about 3-7ths of E. The northern countries furnish good materials for the carpenter and ship-builder. The forest-trees of the warmer climate are the tamarisk, carube, sumach, mastic, cork, plane, sycamore, and cypress. The flora of the extreme south of E. is analogous to that of Africa. Mineral kingdom.] Nearly all the metals, and even several of the more precious minerals, are found in Europe. Population.] It is difficult to estimate the precise amount of the pop. of E., notwithstanding the accuracy with which the census of most European states is now taken; for we do not possess a census of contemporary surveys; and in Turkey the pop. can only be loosely estimated from the number of hearths paying tax. In 1787, Zimmerman estimated the pop. of E. at 144,000,000. In 1828, according to Malte Brun, it was probably not overrated at 205,000,000; while Schlieben estimated it at only 189,000,000. The Weimar Almanac for 1837, estimated it at 232,200,649; in 1844, at 241,252,653. It must now exceed 260,000,000. This pop. is not equally concentrated throughout Europe. Thus, in Iceland and Färoe it is only 12 to the sq. m.; in the Netherlands generally it is as 342; and in E. Flanders nearly double that number; in England alone it is, according to the census of 1841, as 297; and over Great Britain and Ireland, as 219; in the European provs. of Russia it is as 28; while over the whole of Europe it is as 61 to the sq. m. Various causes affect the density of pop. and its rate of increase in any country; but upon the whole, the S of Europe is more populous than the N in proportion to its extent; and must continue so, while the means of subsistence are procured with so much greater facility in southern countries. The mass of this population consists neither of insulated nations nor of closely related tribes. The leading nations, according to Hassel, are:- 1st, The GERMAN NATIONS, predominant in Germany, the Netherlands, Switzerland, Great Britain, Denmark, Norway, Sweden, and Eastern Prussia. All these nations speak different dialects of the German language. Their total number in E. is estimated by Dr. Kombst at 77,000,000. 2d, The Roman nations, the descendants of those tribes which overthrew the Roman Western empire, and settling in the subjugated countries, adopted the Roman or Latin language, which they modelled upon their own dialects. Hence arose the mixed languages called Roman dialects, spoken at this day by the French, Italians, Spaniards, Portuguese, and Wallachians. Dr. Kombst, assuming the Celtic type for these nations, estimates their number at 64,000,000. 3d, The Slavonian nations, divided into numerous tribes whose languages are kindred dialects. They occupy the eastern part of E. generally; and consist of the Russians Proper; the Poles, Lithuanians, Letts, and Kurians; the Kassubes in Pomerania; the Wendes in Pomerania and Silesia; the Bohemians; the Slawakes in Moravia and Hungary; the Croatians: the Reizes in Hungary and Russia; the Morlaks in Dalmatia and the Ionian islands; the Montenegrians in Turkey; and the Bothnians and the Uskoches in Dalmatia. Dr. Kombst estimates the European pop. of Slavonic blood at 54,000,000. 4th, The FINNIC NATIONS, including the Finns Proper, the Esthonians, the Livonians, the Lapps, and a mixed race called Magyars, inhabiting Hungary and Transylvania, and by several viewed as a distinct nation. Dr. Kombst estimates the nations of Finnic descent at 30,000,000; and those of Magyar blood at 9,000,000. 5th, The TARTABIC NATIONS, divided into the following branches: the Turks; the Bulgarians, considered by some as Slavonians; the Tartars of Kasan; the Taurian Tartars; and the Nogaians in Cherson and the Crimea. Besides these nations, we may enumerate many distinct races in Europe. Such are the Greeks, the Albanians, the Cimmerians, the Caledonians, the Basques, the Maltese, the Circassians, the Samoiedes, and the three scattered tribes of Armenians, Jews, and Gypsies. Religious sects.] The nations assuming to themselves the title of Christian, in Europe, are divided into three leading sects: viz. 1st, the Greek, or Eastern Church, which prevails in Greece and the Ionian islands, part of Albania and Bulgaria, in Servia, Slavonia, Wallachia, Moldavia, and Russia; and has numerous adherents in Austria, Turkey, and the two Sicilies. The number of members belonging to it, in Europe, amounts to from 50,000,000 to 60,000,000. 2d, The Latin, or Roman Catholic church, of which the Pope is the head. This creed is predominant in Italy, Spain, Portugal, France, Austria, the half of Germany and of Switzerland, the Southern Netherlands, Russian Poland, and Ireland, and numbers some adherents in Great Britain, Holland, and Turkey. The total number of Roman Catholics in E. amounts to about 140,000,000. 3d, The Protestant church, which predominates under different creeds, in Denmark, Sweden, Norway, Great Britain, Prussia, a part of Germany and of Switzerland; and has also numerous professors in Hungary, Transylvania, and France. Its principal branches are the Lutheran, the Presbyterian, and the Episcopalian church, and it may embrace about 58,000,000. Mahommedanism, or Islamism, is professed by the Turks and other Tartarian hordes, the Circassians, and a part of the Arnauts. Its European votaries have been estimated at about 8,000,000. The cultivation of the soil, therefore, has been carried to great perfection in this part of the earth; especially in the British empire, the Netherlands, Switzerland, Germany, some parts of Italy, Denmark, and Sweden; although Russia, Hungary, and Poland, whose agriculture is not nearly so advanced, are still the granaries of E. The rearing of cattle is in some countries pursued only in connection with agriculture; in mountainous districts it forms the principal branch of rural industry. The cultivation of fruits belongs to the temperate districts, particularly France and Germany; but the finer fruits can be extensively reared only in the southern parts of E. The growth and manufacture of wine is most considerable in France, the S of Germany, Hungary, Spain, Portugal, and Italy. The olive belongs only to the warmer regions, but different vegetable oils are produced in the temperate parts of E. The rearing of silk-worms is peculiar to warmer climates, and is chiefly carried on in Lombardy. The cultivation of forests has been greatly neglected in most countries; and a very sensible want of wood begins to be felt, although E. is, on the whole, well-stocked with wood. On the subject of railways in E., an article by Dr. F. Von Reden, which appeared in the Allgemeine Zeitung towards the end of 1845, and which attracted much attention, may be advantageeously quoted. "Nearly all the great roads for the conveyance of the commerce of the world," says Dr. Von Reden, "have, within the last century, been diverted into a different direction. The chief causes have been the discovery of new roads,—the change that has taken place in the preponderance of one country over another in the government of the commercial empire,-the opening of new markets for commerce, -and the improvement in the modes of conveyance. The old commercial roads to the East Indies and to Asia have retired into the back-ground since the discovery of the passage round the cape of Good Hope; and that way is now beginning to lose its importance through the road across the isthmus of Suez. The Venetians and Genoese had fixed the chief seat of the commercial world in the S of E.; but through the great maritime power of the Portuguese and Spaniards, the Pyrenean peninsula saw itself-though for a short time only-in the sole possession of the commercial government, which was next transferred to the Dutch, who were, however, soon relieved of the greater part by England. Each of these changes was necessarily the cause of important alterations in the direction of the commercial roads. The opening of new commercial markets, for example, in the colonies formerly belonging to Spain, in Oceania, in China, increased greatly the importance of some roads, while it diminished that of others; but no occurrence has had a greater influence in this matter than the discovery of steam and its application to locomotion. It may be of general interest if I attempt to describe the present and the future prospects of locomotion in E. "As long as England, Holland, France, and Germany, remain in possession of the greater portion of the world's commerce, and as long, on the other hand, as Southern and Middle Asia, the fifth quarter of the globe, and the E coast of Africa, continue to justify the great hopes they have raised in the commercial world of E., so long the roads the most important for commerce will traverse, in a SE and NW direction, the continent of E. The communication of E. with America, and with the E coast of Africa, will naturally always be by sea; although the time, without doubt, is not far distant when the two oceans will be connected by a railway either across the isthmus of Panama, or even through California and Florida. As long as no railway is constructed between the Persian and the Black seas, the great commercial road across the isthmus of Suez need fear no great alteration; and Alexandria will, it is probable, be called upon to expedite, for many years to come, the traffic between E. and the Indian seas. Alexandria can now be reached by steamers from Hong-Kong in from 39 to 42 days; from Calcutta, in from 24 to 26 days; from Bombay, in from 14 to 16 days; and from Aden, in from 8 to 10 days. From Alexandria to the N and middle parts of E., especially to the important shores of the North sea, the roads are at present, and most probably will be in future, those commencing at Trieste, Genoa and Marseilles. From Alexandria to Trieste the passage by a steamer occupies 144 or 150 hours; from Alexandria to Genoa, 192 or 200 hours; from Alexandria to Marseilles, 200 to 216; from Alexandria by Malta, Gibraltar, Cadiz, Lisbon, Oporto, and Vigo, to Southampton, 384 to 480. The journey from Madras by Ceylon, the Mauritius, the Cape, St. Jago, Gibraltar, and Southampton, has hitherto occupied 55 or 56 days, or from 1,320 to 1,560 hours, reckoning the necessary stoppages. More or less saving of time seems possible on all these roads, especially by proper rules with respect to the quarantine." In fact, a considerable saving has already been accomplished on all these routes, as will appear by consulting our articles on ALEXANDRIA, CALCUTTA, CHAGRES, HONG-KONG, TRIESTE, SUEZ, and SOUTHAMPTON. [Railway Record.] Political State, and Divisions.] In various states of E., freedom is established beyond the reach of accident or caprice; and even in those governments which in theory are more arbitrary or altogether absolute, the diffusion of knowledge, the state of society and manners, and the force of public opinion, has rendered the ruling power comparatively mild, and attentive to justice and the rights of the subject; and the operation of these causes is extended, and the administration of different governments in some degree assimilated, by that close and intimate union subsisting amongst its various states, which is peculiar to modern E. In ancient times, and in other divisions of the globe, every independent state is unconnected and detached; it looks on an inactive spectator, while one of its neighbours is overrunning and conquering another; and interferes not, unless for the defence or extension of its own territories or dominion. In this state of things, some one individual power is always acquiring an undue preponderance and ascendancy fatal to the independence of the rest; and the face of the political world is perpetually undergoing great and sudden changes. But the similarity in situation of the E. powers,—the resemblance of languages, manners, and laws,—the extension of intercourse by travelling and foreign residence,—their union, by the relations of scientific and commercial pursuits,—the universal diffusion of some form of the Christian religion,—and a conviction of the great and common advantages of union,-have given birth, among the states of E., to an intimate connection in times of peace, and a common feeling of interest in maintaining the existence of the present state of affairs. Each power views with a certain measure of solicitude the dangers which beset the rest, and feels itself attacked when any of the weaker their more formidable neighbours. Holland, . 800,000,000<br>England, . 5,556,000,000 Progressive geography.] The progressive geography of E. will be more properly and more minutely illustrated under the descriptions of its different kingdoms and states. Homer was acquainted with the coasts of the Ægean sea, and Phœnician navigators had reached the south-western coasts of England several centuries before the Christian era. Spain, France, and Britain were early and fully explored by the Romans, who overran them with their arms, and established in them permanent settlements. Their ships explored the southern shores of the Baltic, as far as the river Rubo, or the Western Dwina, and discovered the names of several tribes along the coasts; they were also acquainted with the southern parts on the left of the Danube, but of the central parts of Germany it is evident, from the maps of Ptolemy, they had no just ideas. Of Scandinavia, the ancients knew only the southern parts, as far as the lakes Wetter and Wenner. The Carpathian or Sarmatian mountains were well-known; but the line of 50° or 52° N lat. bounded the ancient knowledge in the north-east, and the wide extent of the Russian empire continued almost unknown to Europe until the sixteenth century. The survey of Europe on trigonometrical principles—which alone can afford data for the construction of accurate maps -is by no means yet completed; nor do we yet possess accurate charts of the whole coasts. But the different European governments are now exerting themselves in the furtherance of these important objects; and it is probable that in a few years we shall be in possession of very satisfactory maps and charts of the entire continent even within the limits of the Arctic circle. History.] The first inhabitants of E. came from Asia, the cradle of the human race; and it is probable that the SE corner of the continent, or Greece, was the region earliest peopled in E. The nation of the Hellenes existed here fourteen centuries before the Christian era, and soon surpassed the primitive Asiatic stock in intellectual culture. The highest point in the civilization of this nation-better known under the appellation of Greeks-may be fixed about three centuries before the birth of Christ. Great in men and deeds, enriched by arts and sciences, adorned with all the most glorious productions of a variously cultivated intellect, this epoch in the history of Greece will ever remain the admiration of mankind. But this ennobled people were destined to experience the instability of all human things. With the dissolution of the universal monarchy which Alexander the Great had erected upon the ruins of Grecian freedom, the Grecian name lost its pre-eminence in the history and fortunes of Europe. At the same moment in which the star of Grecian ascendancy sunk beneath the political horizon of E., there arose, in a neighbouring country, another state which was destined to win the empire of the world. On the banks of a small Italian stream, a petty colony from Asia had early located themselves. These strangers gradually associated with the surrounding tribes, and at last united with them in a regularly constituted community. Thus was founded infant Rome. Having made themselves masters of the whole of Italy, and successfully terminated the struggle with Carthage for the dominion of the countries bordering on the Mediterranean, the Romans began to spread their name and intia, the Hungarian provinces of Pannonia, Illyria, and Dacia, under Charlemagne, that the new states of France, Germany, Italy, Burgundy, Lorraine, and Navarre, were now created out of it. About the same period, the Slavonians founded kingdoms in Bohemia, Poland, Russia, and the north of Germany; and, in the extreme North, the gallant Normans became conspicuous in European history. The Popes of Rome at last succeeded in establishing pretensions for which the minds of men had been wellprepared by their religious instructors. Under Gregory the papal hierarchy first presented a new form of absolute monarchy in Europe. Its dominion was in the minds of the people; and the tools with which it operated upon society were the strongest feelings of human nature. Hence the ease with which a few priests excited the warlike powers of Europe to the mad expeditions against the infidel possessors of the Holy Land. The Crusades swept away millions of men, but were the means of introducing the arts and sciences of the more polished East into Europe, and ultimately gave rise to a middle and most beneficial class of society, which formed the connecting link between the nobles or lords of the soil, and their peasantry or bondsmen.- The darkness of the Middle ages at last began to clear up. Italy, the centre of the hierarchical power and opulence, and a country favourably situated for the commerce of the Mediterranean, was at once enriched by the influx of the wealth of Europe, and invited again to take the lead in the arts of civilized life. Commerce, with the same mighty influence which she had anciently exerted, subdued the spirit of barbarism upon all the coasts of E., and the dawn of modern civilization and refinement arose in her horizon. The invention of the art of printing was soon and necessarily followed by the Reformation; and from these grand and primary causes have resulted a thousand principles, by the combined operation of which E. has become to the rest of the world, what the heart is to the human body, or the sun to the planetary system.
EURVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Chevillon. Pop. 665- It has extensive iron-works.

EURYTANIA, a diocese or administrative prov. of Greece, bounded on the N and W by Turkey, on the E by the dio. of Phthiotis, and on the S by those of Etolia and Trichonia. Its W boundary is formed by the Aspropotamo, and it is intersected by several large streams which flow into that river. Pop. in 1839, 21,533. Its capital is Karpenisi. It is generally mountainous, and near its E confines is Mount Velonki, which rises to the height of 7,544 ft.
EUSEBE (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Mont-Saint- Vincent, 22 m. SW of Chalons-sur-Saone. Pop. 1,010.
EUSKIRCHEN, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency and 23 m. SSW of Cologne, near the Erft. Pop. 3,100. It has manufactories of cloth and soap.
EUSSERTHAL, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of the Pfalz, NW of Landau.
EUSTACHE (SAINT), a village of Lower Canada, at the mouth of the Riviere du Chene, 15 m. NW of Montreal. Pop. 950.

EUSTATIUS (SAINT), or SAN EUSTAQUIO, an island of the Little Antilles, in the group of the Leeward islands, 13 m. NW of St. Christopher, and about the same distance SE of the island of Saba, in N lat. 17° 30', and W long. 63° 4'. It is 5 m. in length from NW to SE, and about 3 m. in average breadth, comprising an area of 190 sq. m. Pop. in 1834, 2,300; in 1847, 1,903. It rises in a pyramidal form, and has in the centre of the cone a depression now covered with luxuriant vegetation, but exhibiting unmistakeable indications of having been the crater of a volcano. The coasts on all sides except the SW, on which the town of St. E. is situated, are steep and inaccessible. The climate is reputed to be healthy, but hurricanes and earthquakes are of frequent occurrence, and water is extremely scarce. The soil is fertile and well-cultivated, producing tobacco in large quantities, sugar, coffee, indigo, maize, yams, and potatoes. Pigs, goats, and fowls are extensively reared for export as well as for local use. This island was formerly the entrepot of an extensive contraband trade in European goods with the adjacent islands and the continent of America. It was first taken possession of by the Dutch in 1635; but subsequently passed several times into the hands of the English and French. It was finally restored to Holland in 1814. The town of St. E. is situated on the SW coast of the island, partly on the shore, and partly on the acclivity of the cone. It is wellbuilt, and is well-supplied with large store-houses. It has no port. The roadstead opposite affords good anchorage, but is exposed to the violence of the S and SW winds. It is defended by two forts, situated on an eminence to the NW, and commanding the approach to the town.
EUSTON, a parish of Suffolk, 3 m. SSE of Thetford, on the Lesser Ouse. Area 3,910 acres. Pop. in 1831, 202; in 1841, 255.
EUTAW, a village of Green co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 3 m. W of Black-Warrior river.

EUTAW SPRINGS, a small river of the state of S. Carolina, U.S., an affluent of the Santee, noted for an engagement which took place on its banks between the English and Americans on the 8th Sept., 1781.

EUTIN, a town of Oldenburg, capital of the principality of Lubeck, on a small lake of the same name, 21 m. N of Lubeck, and 9 m. from the shore of the gulf of that name. Pop. 2,816. It possesses a castle, and a modern grand ducal palace, situated on an island of the lake. It has also a Lutheran church, a college, a classical and an industrial school, and an hospital. Fairs are held six times a-year.
EUTROPE-DE-BORN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Villereal, 15 m. N of Villeneuve-sur-Lot. Pop. 1,945.
EUTROPE-DE-LA-LAND (SAINT), a village of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. and 2 m. NN W of Montmoreau. Pop. 274. It has manufactories of pottery and an annual fair.
EUTZSCH, a village of Prussian Saxony, circle of Merseburg, S of Wittenberg. Pop. 279.
EUVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. of Commercy. Pop. 596. It has quarries of free-stone.
EUXINE SEA. See BLACK SEA.
EUXTON, a chapelry in the p. of Leyland, Lancashire, 2 m. WNW of Chorley, intersected by the Wigan and Preston railway. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 1,562.
EVAL (St.), a parish of Cornwall, 42 m. WNW of St. Columb-Major. Area 2,970 acres. Pop. 349.

EVANGELISTS, a group of small desert islands, in the South Atlantic ocean, near the SW coast of Patagonia, and at the W entrance of the strait of Magalhaens. They are 4 in number; and, with the 8 islands which lie 16 m. to the W, form the group of the Twelve Apostles. The southernmost is in S lat. 52° 24′ 18", and W long. 75° 02′ 56"; and bears S 38° E, 23½ m. from Cape Pillar.—Stokes.

EVANS, a township of Erie co., in the state of EVE New York, U. S., 311 m. W of Albany; bounded on the W by Lake Erie, and drained by Little and Big Sister's creeks. Its surface is hilly, and its soil gravelly loam and clay. Pop. in 1840, 1,807.
EVANS, a river of South Eastern Australia, which flows into the ocean in S lat. 29° 10'.
EVANS' MILLS, a village of Le Ray township, Jefferson co., in the state of New York, U. S., 165 m. NW of Albany, on Pleasant creek, an affluent of Indian river. Pop. in 1840, 350.

EVANSVILLE, a village of Preston co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 266 m. NW of Richmond, between Cheat and Valley rivers.—Also a village of Vanderburg co., in the state of Indiana, U. S., 172 m. SSW of Indianapolis, on the N bank, and at the great bend of the Ohio river, at the confluence of Green river. Pop. in 1840, 2,500.
EVANTON, a village in the p. of Kiltearn, co. of Ross and Cromarty. Pop. 462.

EVAUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, arrond. of Aubusson. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,747; in 1841, 10,742.—The town, which is very ancient, is 24 m. NE of Aubusson, and stands on a height. Pop. in 1841, 2,698. It is noted for its thermal baths, and in the environs are the ruins of the castle of La Roche-Aymon. It has extensive tanneries, and a trade in coarse linen, grain, and cattle.
EVECHE'S (TROIS), formerly a district of France, in the prov. of Lorraine. It comprised the three small bishoprics of Messin, Verdunois, and Toulois.
EVEDON, a parish of Lincolnshire, 23 m. NE of Sleaford. Area 2,300 acres. Pop. 91.
EVEGNEE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege. Pop. 220.
EVELETTE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur. Pop. 821.
EVENIG, a village of Norway, in the dio. of North Berghuus, on a peninsula to the S of the Sogne-fiord.
EVENJOBB, a township in the p. of Old Radnor, Radnorshire, 5 m. NW of Kington. Pop. 345.
EVENLOAD, or EVENLODE, a parish of Worcestershire, 4 m. E of Moreton-in-the-Marsh. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. 325.—Also a river in Oxfordshire, which falls into the Isis about 5 m. NW of Oxford.
EVENLY, or Evenley, a parish of Northamptonshire, 1 m. S of Brackley, and to the S of the Ouse. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 487.

EVENUS, a river of Greece, in Livadia, which takes its rise in the NE part of the diocese of Entolia; runs W to the confines of the diocese of Trichonia; and unites with the Phidaris at the S extremity of that diocese.
EVENWOOD, a township in the p. of St. Andrew- Auckland, co. of Durham, and S of the Gaunless. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. included in that of Barony.
EVERBECQ, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 27 m. NE of Tournai. Pop. 4,237.
EVERBERGH, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant. Pop. 1,077.
EVERCREECH, a parish of Somerset, 33 m. SSE of Shepton-Mallet. Area 3,130 acres. Pop. 1,449.
EVERDON, a parish of Northamptonshire, 4 m. SSE of Daventry. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 777.
EVERETTSVILLE, a village of Albemarle co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 78 m. WNW of Richmond.
EVERGHEM, a canton and commune of Belgium, in the prov. of East Flanders, arrond. and 4 m. N of Gand. Pop. 1,077. Boats of small size are built here.
EVERINGHAM, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. WNW of Market Weighton. Area 3,080 acres. Pop. 318.
EVERLEY, or EVERLEIGH, a parish of Wilts, 43 354. See also Suffield-Cum-Everley.
EVERSBERG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, regency and 12 m. ESE of Arnsberg, on the Rhur. Pop. 1,030.
EVERSDEN (GREAT), a parish of Cambridgeshire, 5 m. SE of Caxton. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. in 1831, 316; in 1841, 300.
EVERSDEN (LITTLE), a parish of Cambridgeshire, 51 m. ESE of Caxton. Area 670 acres. Pop. in 1831, 196; in 1841, 225.
EVERSEM, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, and cant. of Meysse. Pop. 260.
EVERSHOLT, a parish of Bedfordshire, 21 m. SE of Woburn. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 899.
EVERSHOT, a parish of Dorsetshire, 74 m. NE of Beaminster. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. 566.
EVERSLAER, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of East Flanders, cant. of Lokeren. Pop. 522.
EVERSLEY, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 2 m. N of Hartford-Bridge. Area 5,400 acres. Pop. in 1831, 755; in 1841, 770.
EVERTHORP. See Drewton.

EVERTON, a parish of Bedfordshire, 43 m. NNE of Biggleswade. Area 975 acres. Pop. 233.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Walton-on-the-hill. Pop. returned with that of the borough of Liverpool.—Also a parish of Nottinghamshire, 3 m. ESE of Bawtry, and N of the Idle. Area 4,630 acres. Pop. 1,094. dep. of Finistere, cant. of Fouesnant, 6 m. SE of Florence. Quimper. Pop. 1,039.
EVESBATCH, a parish of Herefordshire, 53 m. SSE of Bromyard. Area 830 acres. Pop. 98.

EVESHAM, a borough and market-town in the co. of Worcester, 923 m. NW of London, and 15 m. SE of Worcester, delightfully situated at the N extremity of the vale of E., on an acclivity rising from a bend in the banks of the Avon, which surrounds it on all sides but the N, and is navigable for vessels of 60 tons, by locks, as high as Stratford. The town principally consists of four or five regular wide streets, with well-built houses. Area of borough, with St. Peter Bengeworth, 2,150 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,937; in 1831, 3,991; in 1841, 4,245. E. sends 2 members to parliament. The number of electors registered in 1837 was 359; in 1848, 371. The boundaries of the new parliamentary borough coincide with those of the old municipal borough, which included the three ps. of All Saints, St. Lawrence, and St. Peter Bengeworth. The suburb of Bengeworth, on the E side of the river, is as large nearly as the town itself, and for political and municipal purposes is united with it. The income of the borough, in 1839, was £424. The only manufacture carried on here is that of stocking-weaving. The principal employment of the labouring poor is that of gardening, large portions of ground on each side of the Avon, in the celebrated vale of E., having been converted into gardens. The produce is sent for sale to Cheltenham, Tewkesbury, Birmingham, and Liverpool. One of the most remarkable and decisive battles in the English annals was fought here on the 6th of August, 1265, between the forces of Henry III., commanded by Prince Edward, afterwards Edward I., and Simon de Montford, earl of Leicester. The issue of the contest was the decisive defeat and death of the earl, the release of the king, and his subsequent reinstatement on the throne.—E. abbey is the principal point of interest in the history and existing features of E. It is stated that at the time of the conquest it possessed 21,862 acres of land. The stately tower of the abbey-church still remains.

EVESHAM, a township of Burlington co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 34 m. S of Trenton. It | S lat. 55° 35', and W long. 66° 40'. EVO m. NW of Ludgershall. Area 3,370 acres. Pop. | has a level surface, and is drained by Rancocus and Haines' creeks, and by the head waters of Little- Egg-Harbour. Its soil consists of a fertile sandy loam, and is well cultivated. Pop. in 1840, 5,060.

EVIAN, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Chablais, 8 m. ENE of Thonon, on the S bank of the lake of Geneva, at an alt. of 1,354 ft. above scalevel. Pop. 2,084. It has 2 parish churches, 2 convents, and a college. It has some trade in chestnuts, kirschwasser, and oil. In the vicinity are the noted chalybeate baths of Amphion.
EVIE RENDALL, a parish forming the NE extremity of the mainland of Orkney, bounded by Enhallow-sound on the E. Pop. in 1811, 1,227; in 1841, 1,518.
EVIE, or EFIE, a town of Norway, in the bail. of Nedenaes, on the l. bank of the Torris, 30 m. N of Christiansand. Pop. 2,553.
EVIERO, a town of the island of Santa-Maura, on the S coast.
EVINGTON, a parish of Leicestershire, 3 m. ESE of Leicester, near the Midland Counties railway. Area 1,360 acres. Pop. 285.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Leigh, Gloucestershire. Pop. 266.
EVISA, a canton and commune of Corsica, in the arrond. of Ajaccio. The cant. comprises 3 com. Pop. in 1831, 1,906; in 1841, 2,176. The village is 26 m. N of Ajaccio.
EVOLA, or EBOLA, a river of Tuscany, which
EVERZAC (SAINT), a commune of France, in the | flows into the Arno, on the l. bank, 25 m. W of

EVOLENA, a parish and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, dixain of Evens, on the Borgne [i. e. Brook or Burn]. Pop. 927, Catholics. Its neighbourhood presents a lively and fertile appearance, the valley, a branch of the Val d'Erin, being broad and well watered, covered with pasture, and studded with barns and châlets to a great height on both sides.

EVORA, a comarca and town of Portugal, cap. of the prov. of Alemtejo, 80 m. E of Lisbon, on a rising ground near the source of the Chamara or Moinhos. Pop. 15,000. It is enclosed by a now dilapidated wall, and has a citadel and two forts, but all in a ruinous condition. The streets are narrow and tortuous, and the houses old and ill-built; but the town is "striking from its elevation, and venerable from its ancient towers." It possesses, however, a fine cathedral, 4 parish-churches, several convents and hospitals, a library, a museum, which is one of the most extensive in Portugal, a college, an episcopal seminary, and excellent barracks. Of its ancient edifices those most worthy of note are the aqueduct, still in a good state of preservation, and the ruins of a temple of Diana, now used as a slaughter-house. Hardware, leather, and hats are the chief articles of local manufacture. — This town, originally called Ebora, was a stronghold of great antiquity. It was named by the Romans Liberalitas Julia, and became the capital of the gov. of Sertorius. In 715 it was taken by the Moors. It decided in favour of Don Miguel in 1828, but was afterwards taken by the constitutional army. It has frequently been the residence of the kings of Portugal.—Also a town in the prov. of Estremadura, comarca and 6 m. ENE of Alcobaça, on the l. bank of the Baca.

EVORA-MONTE, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 12 m. WNW of Villa-Viçosa, and 20 m. NE of Evora, on a hill. Pop. 800. It has an old castle and two churches. The convention of May 26th, 1834, by which Don Miguel made formal renouncement of the throne of Portugal, was held here.

EVOUT'S ISLAND, a small island in the South Atlantic ocean, to the E of Wollaston island, in EVPATORIA, JEVPATORIA, or EUPATORIA, formerly called Koslev or Kaslof, and the Pompeiopolis of the Romans, a district and town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Taurida, 42 m. NW of Simferopol, on a bay of the Black sea. Pop. 9,460, chiefly Tartars and Jews. It is surrounded by walls now in ruins, and defended by an old fortress. It has several mosques, amongst which that built by Devlet-Ghiri-Khan in 1552 is a structure of great magnificence. There are also an Armenian and a Greek church, a synagogue, a high Tartar school, several medressehs, numerous khans or store-houses, a custom-house, 2 public baths, numerous coffeehouses, and an hospital. The port is small and shallow. The roadstead affords good anchorage, but, except on the N and NE, is destitute of shelter. It has, however, a considerable trade, consisting in salt, grain, bar-iron, wool, leather, cloth, skins, and butter. Quarantine is enforced upon all vessels coming from the Levant. The town is supplied with water by means of an artesian well. In the vicinity are two saline lakes, which annually afford an immense quantity of salt .- E. received its name from its founder, Mithridates Eupator, king of Pontus. Under the Tartar domination it was one of the most important and most populous towns in the gov. It was first taken by the Russians in 1736, again in 1771, and ultimately, in 1783, became with the Crimea incorporated with the Russian dominions. The district occupies the north portion of the peninsula. It is 72 m. in length, and about 39 in breadth. It is watered by numerous small rivers, and contains several large salt lakes. Its soil is fertile, and it is generally well-cultivated.

EVRAN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Côtes-du-Nord, arrond. of Dinan. The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,701; in 1841, 10,624. The town is 7 m. SSE of Dinan, on the r. bank of the Rance. Pop. in 1841, 1,841.
EVRANGE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Moselle, cant. of Cattenom. Pop. 187. It has a custom-house.

EVRE', a river of France, which takes its rise in the marshy district of Bourdelins, to the SW of Nerondes; runs under the name of Jevrette to Bourges, where it receives the Auron and takes the appellation of Ivre; thence continues in a NW direction, passes Mehun and Vierzon, and a little below the latter town joins the Cher after a total course of nearly 45 m.

EVRECY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Caen. The cant. comprises 28 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,928; in 1841, 12,633. The town is 9 m. SW of Caen. Pop. 839. It has a manufactory of oil.
EVREGNIES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault. Pop. 878.
EVREHAILLES, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur. Pop. 726.
EVREO-CASTRO, a village of Greece, in Livadia, in the diocese of Etolia, 6 m. NW of Naupactus or Lepanto, and near the l. bank of the Phidaris. It occupies the site of the ancient Calydon.

EVREUX, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Eure.—The arrond, comprises an area of 203,086 hectares, and contains 10 cant.; viz. Saint-André, Breteuil, Couches, Damville, Evreux, Nonancourt, Pacy-sur-Eure, Rugles, Verneuil, and Vernow. Pop. in 1831, 118,396; in 1846, 121,793.—The cant. comprises 53 com. Pop. in 1831, 23,820; in 1841, 26,269.—The town, which is the capital of the dep., is situated in a valley, on the Iton, which here divides into 3 branches, 32 m. S of Rouen, and 60 m. WNW of Paris, in N lat. 49° 1' 30", and E long. 3° 40'. Pop. in 1789, 5,772; in EWH 1821, 9,729; in 1831, 9,963; and in 1841, 11,716. It is a large and well-built town, and possesses many handsome edifices, of which the principal are the cathedral, a fine Gothic structure, with a spire 265 ft. in height, the episcopal palace, the abbey of St. Tauren founded in 660, the town-hall, and the theatre. It has also a communal college, a public library of 10,000 vols., several seminaries, a savings' bank, 3 printing-establishments, and a botanic garden. The principal manufactures of E. are cloth, silk and cotton fabrics, and hosiery. It has also cotton and wool-spinning mills, several tanneries, dye-works, bleacheries, copper and zinc works, oil, tan, and corn mills, sawmills, and lime, tile, and gypsum kilns. The trade consists chiefly in grain, timber, horses, and cattle. Fairs are held 7 times a-year. The surrounding district produces grain, fruit, and in small quantities wine.—This town is supposed to occupy the site of the Mediolanum or Eburovices of the Romans. It was sacked in 892 by the Normans, again in 962 by Lothaine, again by Amaury-de-Montfort, and in 1119 by Henry I. of England. It was taken repeatedly by the French and English until 1441, when the former were confirmed in its possession. In 1649 it was taken by the duke de Longueville.

EVRON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, arrond. of Laval. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,457; in 1841, 15,272. The town is situated in a marshy district 20 m. ENE of Laval. Pop. in 1841, 4,130. It has a magnificent alms-house—formerly a Benedictine abbey—a handsome town-hall, and a college; and possesses manufactories of linen, plain and damask, and thread. It has 10 annual fairs and a considerable trade in linen, wool, thread, grain, and poultry.
EVROULT-DE-MONTFORT (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Gacé, 24 m. E of Argantan. Pop. 929. It has several iron-works.
EWANOWITZ, or EWANOWICE, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Moravia, circle and 25 m. ENE of Brünn, on the l. bank of the Hanna. Pop. 1,780.
EWART, a township in the p. of Doddington, Northumberland, 5 m. NNW of Wooler, between the rivers Till and Glen. Pop. 176.

EWATTINGEN, a parish and village of the grand duchy of Baden, circle of the Lake, bail. and 5 m. NE of Bonndorf, and 40 m. WNW of Constanz, near the l. bank of the Wutach. Pop. 729 (Cath.). It has the remains of an ancient castle.
EWE, or Eve, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 100 m. ESE of Revel, and 30 m. W of Narva.—Also a town in the gov. and 18 m. WNW of Vilna.
EWE (SAINT), a parish in Cornwall, 3 m. ENE of Tregoney. Area 6,100 acres. Pop. 1,468.
EWELL, a parish in Surrey, 5 m. SSE of Kingston, on the road to Worthing. Area 4,170 acres. Pop. 1,867.—Also a parish in Kent, 3 m. NW of Dover. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 392.
EWELME, a parish of Oxfordshire, 3\frac{1}{4} m. ENE of Wellingford. Area 2,170 acres. Pop. 663.
EWENNY, a parish of Glamorganshire, on the Wenny, 3 m. W of Cowbridge. Pop. 211. It is intersected by a river of the same name, an affluent of the Ogmore.
EWERBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 41 m. NE of Sleaford. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. 433. It contains the hamlet of Ewerby-Thorpe. Pop. 48.
EWES, a parish of Dumfries-shire, on a small river of the same name, 4 m. N of Langholm. Area 344 sq. m. Pop. 328.
EWESLEY, a township in the parochial chapelry of Nether-Wilton, Northumberland. Pop. 20.

FWHURST, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 6 m. NW of Basingstoke. Area 820 acres. Pop. 22.—Also a parish of Surrey, 83 m. SW of Dorking. Area 4,390 acres. Pop. 942.—Also a parish in Sussex, 4 m. NE of Roberts-Bridge, on the S bank of the Rother. Area 5,310 acres. Pop. 1,169.
EWING, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 309.—Also a township of Mercer co., in the state of New Jersey. Pop. 1,017.
EWINGTON, a village of Effingham co., in the state of Illinois, 85 m. SE of Springfield, on the National road, on the W bank of Little Wabash river.
EWILLE-LES-PARQUIAUX (DE L'), a commune of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, cant. of Templeuve. Pop. 290.
EWLOE TOWN AND EWLOE WOOD, townships in the p. of Hawarden, Flintshire, 6 m. SSE of Flint. Pop. of former, 1,049; of latter, 355.
EWRENKUS, a village of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Rumelia, pash. of Visa, E of Shatal Burgas.
EWRENTI, a village of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Rumelia, pash. of Visa, W of Ashlar.
EWSHOTT, a tything in the p. of Crondale, 4 m. E of Oldham, co. of Southampton. Pop. 646.
EWST, a river of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Livonia, an affluent of the Dwina.
EWYAS-HAROLD, a parish of Herefordshire, 12 m. SW of Hereford, on a branch of the Monnow, and on the line of the Abergavenny and Hereford railway. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 356.
EWYAS, or EVIAS. See TEFFONT.
EXAERDE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of East Flanders, 9 m. NW of Termonde. Pop. 4,134. It has some manufactories of cotton and linen fabrics.
EXALTAÇÃO, a Spanish mission in Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, on the l. bank of the Mamoré, near the junction of the Iruamé.—Also a river of Bolivia, an affluent of Lake Rogualo.
EXBOURNE, a parish of Devonshire, 44 m. SE of Hatherleigh, and E of the Okement. Area 1,600 acres. Pop. 593.
EXBURY, a parish in the co. of Southampton, 7 m. NE of Lymington, and NE of the Beaulieu. Area 2,600 acres. Pop. 406.
EXCHEQUER, or CHESS-BOARD ISLANDS, a group of small islands in the South Pacific, to the N of New Guinea, in S lat. 2° 25', and E long. 144° 10'.

EXCIDEUIL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, arrond. of Périgueux. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,435; in 1841, 10,987. The town is on the r. bank of the Isle, 24 m. NE of Perigueux. Pop. in 1841, 1,907. It has a paper-mill, manufactories of hardware and of mirrors, and numerous ironworks; and possesses a considerable trade in wine and truffles.

EXE, the principal stream in the S of Devonshire. It takes its rise in Exmoor in Somersetshire; flows SE to Exton; turns S and touches on Devonshire about 17 m. from its source; enters Devonshire a little below the junction of the Barle, and flows in a circuitous route to Tiverton, where it expands into a fine river; winds through the vale of Buckleigh; passes Exeter over a schist formation; below that city enters the new red sandstone formation, and passing through fertile meadows reaches Topshain; and flowing between Lympstone and Powderham, over the bar at Exmouth of red sandstone rock, empties itself into the English channel, after a course of about 60 m., with a fall of 1 in 408.—The Exe receives the Barle, the Batham, the Loman, the Culm, the Dart, the Creedy, the Kenn, and the Clyst. The tideway is about 7 m. in length by low writer channel. The extreme difference between spring and neap tides varies from 5 to 7 ft., the tides being much affected by the wind.

EXETER, a city and county of itself, the seat of the see of E., and cap. of the co. of Devon, locally situated in the hund. of Wanford, 173 m. W by S of London by coach-road, and 194 m. by the Great Western, and Bristol and Exeter railways; 44 m. NE of Plymouth; 10 m. NW of Exmouth; and 751 m. by railway from Bristol; on the E side of the river Exe; and 9 m. NW of its embouchure, in 3° 41' W long., and 50° 45' N lat. Area of city 1,800 acres; of city and co. 4,056. Houses in 1841, 5,122. Pop. in 1801, 16,827; in 1811, 18,896; in 1821, 23,479; in 1831, 28,242; in 1841, 31,312, being an increase of 10.97 per cent. from 1831 to 1841. The pop. of the parliamentary burgh in 1841 was 37,231. The situation of E. is commanding and picturesque. It stands on a flat ridge rising about 150 ft. above high-water level of the Exe, and on the SW and NW rather precipitous. Around the SW side flows the Exe; over which, -at the W entrance to the city, a little above the site of the original ancient bridge built in 1250,—a stone-bridge of 3 arches was erected in 1776-8, at the expense of £20,000. The general character of the surrounding scenery is that of a succession of small undulations, increasing in height as they recede from the city, and eventually lost in the eminences which bound the horizon, excepting to the SE, where the estuary of the Exe opens to the English channel. On the N are the Whitstone hills, rising to the height of 740 ft. The Stoke-range connects these with the Woodbury hills to the eastward. On the SW is Haldonhill, exceeding 800 ft. in alt.; and beyond that the lofty ridge of Dartmoor, whose mean height is 1,792 ft. The city is chiefly built on the new red sandstone. In the neighbourhood, trap rock plentifully occurs. The Exe, immediately below the city-walls, is 120 ft. in breadth, by 9 ft. in average depth. The remains of its walls, and many of its streets and buildings, still invest E. with the features of antiquity; although its handsome squares and terraces, and well-built modern streets and houses, plainly show it to be not an ancient city in decay. The space within the ancient walls is nearly in the form of a parallelogram, of 4 furlongs in length, and 3 in breadth. This space is intersected, in a cruciform manner, by four principal streets, which meet near the centre, and diverge at right angles; other streets branch out from these; and the suburbs are principally continuations of them; though suburban streets extend in every direction from the walls. Towards the S the v. of Heavitree, immediately contiguous to the suburbs, consists of houses and villas occupied by retired officers, professional men, and persons connected with the city. The town is lighted with gas, is well-paved, and is now well supplied with water. Within the district of the city, the soil is plentifully perforated by springs; and water is freely met with at from 15 to 30 ft. in depth.—Besides the cathedral, and the other churches, the sessions-house, guildhall, city and county jails and bridewell, the new markets recently opened, and the interesting remains of Rougemont castle, occupying the highest eminence in the city, towards the N, and supposed to have been founded and inhabited by the West Saxon kings, and afterwards the seat of the dukes of Exeter, E. possesses various buildings, institutions, or establishments, for the amusement, instruction, or convenience of the public. Among these are a theatre and circus; a spacious assembly or ball-room called the Devon and Exeter subscription-rooms; public baths at Southernhay; a public library in the cathedral, denominated the Devon and Exeter institution, established in 1817, and containing about 10,000 vols.; the Athenæum, a literary institution established in 1835, when a handsome lectureroom was erected; a Mechanics' institute established in 1825; and a literary and philosophical society founded in 1836. Many residents are attracted to E. by the salubrity of the climate, the excellence of the locality, and the cheapness of provisions. "The chief characteristics of the climate of E.," says Dr. Shapter, in his valuable report on the sanatory condition of E., "are those of being warm, soft, mild, equable, calm, and free from storms; and though subject to a large share of rain, it seldom occurs that a whole day is so unceasingly wet as not to afford some hours for out-door exercise." The average mortality of the whole city in 1841 was 2.45, or one in every 40.79; in 1800 it was 2.58. The average mortality in the best districts in 1841 was 1.28; in the worst or poor districts 2.69. Henry constituted E. a distinct co. by itself; thus rendering it independent of Devon, of which it is nevertheless the cap. The co. comprises, in addition to the ancient city, the ps. of St. Sidwell, St. David, and St. Edmund's, with the greater part of the ps. of the Holy Trinity and St. Mary-steps. E. is said to have been one of the first cities that returned members to parliament; at all events, it has returned 2 members ever since the reign of Edward I. The right of election was vested in the freemen and resident freeholders, of whom there were supposed to have been about 1,200. The number of names on the register for 1836 was 3,488, of whom 460 were freemen, 952 freeholders, and 2,076 occupiers; in 1848 the number of electors was 3,798. The limits of the municipal boundary now coincide with those of the new and extended parliamentary borough. The net income of the borough for 1839 was £12,882. The judges on the western circuit hold the assizes for the county-of-the-city twice a-year at the guildhall, and twice a-year for the co. of Devon at the sessions-house. The sessions-house is a handsome building, faced with Portland stone, within the area of Rougemont castle-yard. County and election meetings, &c., are held in the open area in front of the building. Below the castle-hill is the countyjail, a large and well-built brick edifice of modern construction. Near it are the cavalry barracks. The city-jail has 10 male and 3 female wards; the house-of-correction, 7 male and 4 female wards. The guildhall, a spacious and convenient structure in the High-street, in the centre of the town, was built in 1593, and repaired in 1720. It is remarkable for the massiveness and variety of its architecture. Trade and manufactures.] The trade of E. is not at present nearly so great as it formerly was. It was calculated, say the Lysons, by an eminent mercantile man resident near E., "that for nearly two centuries previous to the Spanish war of 1796, the average annual amount of the exports had been £300,000, besides the sales to London and the East India company of serges and other articles, which were probably about the same amount." The exports in 1822 did not exceed £60,000 per annum, exclusive of the East India trade, which was calculated at £200,000. The principal exports are serges and other woollen goods, paper, and manganese: the imports are general merchandise,-including groceries from London, -timber, coals, chiefly from Newcastle, and limestone. The gross receipt of customs-duty at E. port for 1838 was £84,406; for 1843, £88,151; for 1846, £97,715. The number of sailing vessels registered at the port of E. on December 31, 1841, was 50 of a total tonnage of 1,637 tons, or under 50 tons burthen; and 131 of a total tonnage of 15,637 tons, and 1 steamer of 17 tons. In the year 1841, 1,079 vessels of 89,135 tons burthen entered coastwise; and 624 of 37,486 tons burthen cleared outwards; 45 steam-vessels of 5,445 tons burthen entered and cleared in the same period. The registered shipping in 1847 was 176 vessels = 16,285 tons.—The principal market-day is Friday, on which is held a great corn market, esteemed the greatest market in the W of England for all kinds of provisions. The market for wool was transferred from Crediton to E. in 1538. The cloth-halls used during the fair for the sale of woollen cloth occupy the cloister under the school and library at St. John's hospital; and new markets have been built, and recently opened, at an expense of £64,000.—The manufactures of this city were formerly of much greater extent than they now are, though E. has always been more celebrated for trade than manufactures. In 1538, a market was established in the city for the sale of wool, yarn, and kerseys. The merchants purchased the woollen goods manufactured throughout the co. of Devon as they came from the loom, and had them milled, dyed, and finished, before they were exported. In 1618, the citizens traded with France, Germany, Italy, Turkey, and the Levant. The manufacture of white serges was very flourishing in the 18th cent.; great quantities of these were dyed and finished at home, but a still greater proportion was sent white to London, to be there dyed and finished for the foreign markets. In 1800, E. is described as surrounded by fields occupied by racks for stretching and drying the newly washed, cleaned, and dyed serges. At this period E. was the great emporium for the thinnest kind of woollen goods which were exported to the continent and the East Indies. In 1822, the manufactures of the city and its immediate vicinity had much diminished, and were of small extent, consisting chiefly of coarse cloths, and employing from 300 to 400 hands. There had been till recently a considerable manufactory of cassimeres and shawls at Exwick; but that, together with a large cotton factory in the Wearfield, had been discontinued. In 1831, the pop., as regarded employment, had ceased to have any distinctive character; yet it appears to have increased at the rate of 20.28 per cent. betwixt 1821 and 1831; and at a still higher ratio, viz., 24.25 betwixt 1811 and 1821. In 1838, there were only 2 woollen-mills, with 122 hands, and about 300 hand-looms, employed by the manufacturers in the city. See Devonshire. Port and Canal.] The river Exe is navigable for vessels of large burthen throughout the length of its estuary to Topsham, a distance of nearly 8 m. The width of this estuary, in some places, is 1½ m. The distance between Topsham and Exeter is 31 m.; but the river is affected by the tide to within a mile of E. Anciently, it appears, vessels of good size had been accustomed to pass up the river to E bridge, and the tides flowed beyond the city; but it was not till after the year 1563, that the canal, one of the most ancient in the empire, permitted lighters of 16 tons burthen, by the aid of sluices, to ascend with their cargoes to Watergate. This "new work or haven," imperfect and inefficient as it was, still ranks its designer, John Trewe, an engineer of Glamorganshire, amongst the first as well as ablest projectors of inland navigation. It was 3 ft. in depth, by 16 ft. wide, with pound locks similar in all essential points to those of the present day. The expense of the undertaking amounted to £5,000. The canal was subsequently enlarged; but being still found only capable of admitting vessels of comparatively small burthen, and subject to the ebb and flow of the river, which, at the entrance rises 13 ft. in spring tides, operations were recommenced in 1825; and the cut was further extended to Turf, a deeper part of the tideway. The canal is now 5 m. in length, and 15 ft. in depth. There is a sea-lock at Turf, near Topsham on the river Exe, 120 ft. long and 30 ft. wide. The entrance is deep enough to admit vessels drawing 10 ft. water even at neap-tides, when there is not water enough for them to proceed to Topsham: in short, any vessels which can pass the bar at Exmouth,—that is, any drawing from 12 to 14 ft. water, or vessels of 500 tons burthen,—are enabled to proceed at once to E., discharge their cargoes and clear out. Two of the larger class vessels are also now enabled to pass easily abreast along the course of the canal. An excellent basin, or floating-dock, opposite the quay at E., completes the whole, preventing vessels losing time in consequence of the canal being so frequently closed, on account of the floods of the river from inundations, to which this city has always been subject.
