Railways ] Near the new basin of the canal is the terminus of the Bristol and E. railway, which commences by a junction with the Great Western railway at Temple-Mead, Bristol, and is carried through Weston-Supermare, Bridgewater, Taunton, Wellington, Sampford, Peverell, Collumpton, and Bradninch, to the W side of E. Its total length is 751 m. With the Great Western railway, it forms a continuous line of 7 ft. gauge, for 194 m. to London. At White-Vale hill near Wellington, it passes through a tunnel nearly 1 m. in length. The South Devon line, 51 m. 72 chains in length, starting from the station of the Bristol and Exeter railway at Exeter, crosses the river and passes down the flat ground on the bank of the Exe, through Starcross, round the point opposite Exmouth, and thence along the coast by Dawlish, to Teignmouth, and up the valley of the Teign to Newton. From Newton by the valley of Abbot's Kerswell, it passes across a ridge at the v. of Dainton and descends to Totness, passing close to that town, and consequently within about 9 m. of Dartmouth and Brixham. From Totness the line passes by South-Brent and Ivy-Bridge, and approaches the towns of Plymouth and Devonport on the N side. It is probable that this line will be pushed on to Falmouth, and from thence by the West Cornwall line to Penzance and St. Ives. Diocese.] The diocese of E. is divided into the 4 archdeaconries of Cornwall, Exeter, Totness, and Barnstaple. It includes the Scilly isles, together with three Devonshire parishes. The number of benefices in the dio. returned to the commissioners in 1831, inclusive of sinecure rectories, but exclusive of benefices annexed to other preferments, was 613, besides 16 not returned. The aggregate amount of the gross incomes of incumbents in the 613 returned benefices was £194,181; average gross income £316. The total number of curates was 323; stipends, included in the incomes of incumbents, £28,759; average £89. The total number of benefices in 1838, without exclusion, was 639, the incumbents in 178 of which were non-resident. About 240 of the parishes in this diocese are impropriate, or in the hands of laymen. —The total amount of the average gross yearly income of the see of Exeter, and of the ecclesiastical preferments for the three years ending 31st December, 1831, was £3,147: nett yearly income £2,713.—The chapter or corporation of the cathedral consists of 24 canons or prebendaries, of whom 9 are canons residentiary, the dean being one. The prebendaries, not being canons residentiary, receive fixed payments of £20 per annum each: the 9 canons residentiary receive each a fixed payment of £69 5s., and divide among them the surplus nett revenue, after payment of all stipends and allowances. The three archdeacons are paid the annual sum of £135 among them. The lecturer has a fixed stipend of £30 out of the revenues. The sum divided among the canons residentiary was, according to the said average, £5,983. The amount of the average gross yearly income of the dean and chapter, or corporation of the cathedral, as a corporation aggregate, in 1831, was 10,438, besides £914 appropriated to the custos and college of vicars choral. The Cathedral, the principal ornament of the city, is a venerable and magnificent edifice, a noble memorial of religious zeal, and highly interesting to the admirer of ancient English architecture. It has neither the picturesque features nor the lofty majesty of either Durham or Lincoln cathedrals; but its high embowered stone roofs, displaying a boldness of design and a lightness of symmetry equal if not superior to any cathedral architecture in the kingdom, -its storied windows, clustered columns, spacious aisles, sepulchral tablets, and numerous statues,—with its various other appropriate appendages,-must excite interesting emotions in every mind endued with taste for architectural science and grandeur. The variety of styles discovered in this edifice, and the ambiguity and silence of our early historians concerning its foundation, have been fruitful themes for various and opposite opinions. By some historians, the most ancient part of the edifice, or rather the first considerable cathedral, is said to have been planned under the direction and patronage of Bishop Warlewast, a Norman, in 1107 or 1112. This original structure was nearly annihilated, during the siege of Exeter, in 1138, when it was plundered and burnt. Bishop Quivil redesigned and partly rebuilt it, in the pointed style, in 1280, and it was brought by successive degrees to its present form and appearance. It is a cruciform edifice, and consists of a nave with two side-aisles; a chapter-house; a choir, with side-aisles; ten chapels, or oratories; a room called the consistory court; and two short transepts formed out of the two ponderous Norman towers which flank the church on the N and S, and which Quivil and his architect daringly and most skilfully converted into transepts, by taking away the inner side of each tower nearly half its height from the ground, and constructing a great arch to support the remaining upper part. In the S tower there are 11 bells, 10 of which are wrung in peal. These unique towers, with the numerous crocketted pinnacles and flying buttresses, and the highly-pitched roof of the nave and choir, have a very imposing effect from many stations southwards of the city: in the meadows, on the NE, too, the cathedral is seen to rise boldly and grandly above surrounding objects. The W front of this cathedral is unlike any other in the kingdom, and far surpasses most of them in beauty. The lower part is adorned with a rich screen, extending beyond PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP PP EXM the walls of the aisles, and, in altitude, about a third of the central pediment. The design is magnificent. In the upper compartments is a series of canopied niches-35 in number-occupied by statues, in various positions, with sacred symbols more numerous and entire than those of any other cathedral. The episcopal throne in the choir is a noble ornament of carved wood, in the Gothic style, 52 ft. high. The cathedral is rich in funeral monuments. The length of the edifice, including St. Mary's chapel, is 408 ft. from east to west, including the walls. The nave is 175 ft. in length, and 76 in breadth, within the walls; the choir, 120 ft. by about 76. The height of the roof, which is supported by 14 columns, from which rise 16 arches, is 69 ft.; and the Norman towers are 130 ft. in height to the top of the battlements. History.] E. is of antiquity so remote that its origin cannot be distinctly ascertained; but there can be no doubt that it was a settlement of the Britons long previous to the Roman invasion. Its ancient British name was Caerwisc, or Caerisc,—also the ancient British name of Usk in Monmouthshire. By the Romans they were both called Isca; and, to distinguish them, the latter had the additional appellation of Silurum, whilst Exeter was called Isca Damnoniorum; under which name it is mentioned by Antoninus in his Itinerary; and by Ptolemy in the 2d cent. It has been its fate to sustain several severe sieges; but the greatest calamities it ever experienced were inflicted by the Danes, who, in the reign of Alfred, in 876, in violation of a solemn treaty, surprised E. At the Norman conquest, E. withstood the authority of William I., who besieged and took it. It was subsequently exposed to hostilities in the reigns of Stephen and Edward IV. Perkin Warbeck, the real or pretended son of Edward IV., landed in Cornwall, assembled an army of 3,000 men, and with this force laid siege to E.; but the citizens, headed by the earl of Devonshire and his son, gallantly defended their city, and obliged him to retreat. The last siege sustained by this city was in the reign of Edward VI., when the proposed changes in religious worship occasioned an alarming insurrection of the inhabitants of Cornwall and Devonshire. During the parliamentary war, E. at first espoused the royal cause; but the city soon fell into the hands of the parliamentarians. It was subsequently taken by Prince Maurice, and became the head quarters of the royalists in the west of England. Charles's queen made it her residence, and her daughter, the duchess of Orleans, was born here. In 1646, it surrendered to General Fairfax, after a blockade of two months, and since that time its history records no very important events. - Among the distinguished natives of this city, were Josephus Iscanus, a Latin poet of the 13th century, and cotemporary with him, Baldwin, archbishop of Canterbury; Sir Thomas Bodley, founder of the Bodleian library; Lord-chancellor King; Mr. Locke, composer of music; Lord Gifford, and Sir Vicary Gibbs.—The Cecil family derive the titles of earl and marquess from E.

EXETER, a township of Penobscot co., in the state of Maine, U.S., 79 m. NNE of Augusta. It is watered by branches of Kenduskeag river, and has a productive soil.—Also a township of Rockingham co., in the state of New Hampshire, 37 m. ESE of Concord. Pop. in 1840, 2,925. It is intersected by a river of the same name, an affluent of the Piscataque, which is navigable to the village for vessels of 500 tons burden. The soil is fertile and well cultivated.—Also a township of Washington co., in the state of Rhode island, 24 m. SW of Providence, drained by Wood river and its branches. It has a hilly surface. Its soil consists of a fertile gravelly loam. Pop. 1,776.—Also a pastoral township of Oswego co., in the state of New York, 77 m. W of Albany, drained by Butternut and Wharton creeks, and bordered on the NE by Cananderaga lake. Its surface is hilly. Pop. 1,423. The village consists of about 15 dwellings.—Also a township of Luzerne co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 129 m. NNE of Harrisburg, bounded on the NE by the Susquehanna, and watered by its affluents Gardener's creek and Cascade run. It is intersected by the Shawney ridge, and is generally hilly. Its soil is extremely fertile. Anthracite coal is found to the E of the mountains. This township is noted for the battle of Wyoming, fought in 1778. Pop. 1,529.—Also a good agricultural township of Monroe co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 235.—Also a village of Morgan co., in the state of Illinois, 47 m. W of Springfield, on Mauvaiseterre river. Pop. about 100.
EXMINSTER, a parish of Devon, 3 m. SSE of Exeter. Area 4,040 acres. Pop. 1,177.

EXMOOR, an extra-parochial liberty in the W of the co. of Somerset, with a small part in Devon. Area 19,270 acres. Pop. in 1821, 113; in 1831, 52; in 1841, 163. Exmoor, with that part of the adjoining country which forms a portion of the same mass of elevated land, extends from the valley of Stogumber and Crowcombe, separating it from the Quantock hills on the E, to the Hangman hills, on the British channel, near Combe-Martin on the W. The highest portion of this elevated tract is Dunkerry-beacon, S of Porlock, which rises to the height of 1,668 ft. above sea-level. On the W, Chapmanbarrows attain an approximative height of 1,540 ft., and Span-head, ENE from High-bray, a height of 1,610 ft. Along the S boundary, the S end of North Molton-ridge rises to an elevation of 1,413 ft., and Haddon-hill, on the E of Dulverton, to 1,140 ft. The hills and cliffs bordering the Bristol-channel, and constituting a portion of the general Exmoor high-land from Mine-head to Combe-Martin, attain somewhat corresponding heights, forming a coast remarkable for its general elevation and the sub-alpine character of some of its valleys, as, for example, those near Linton. North-hill, on the coast near Minehead, attains a height of 1,059 ft. Culbornehill, near Porlock, is 1,211 ft.; Hall-hill, Countesbury, near Linton, 1,145 ft.; the hill on the W of Trentishoe-barrow, 1,187 ft.; and the Great Hangman-hill, near Combe-Martin, 1,083 ft. above the level of the sea. [De la Beche.] Some of these hills are cultivated; others, marsh or waste lands covered with fern or wild thyme. On the whole, E. is a dreary, wild, and sterile range of country. It was originally a forest, the wood of which is said to have been consumed at the ancient iron-works of Exford, on its borders; but a thorn bush, or a few straggling willows on the margin of a brook, are all that it now affords in the shape of trees. Mr. Pusey has recently called the attention of agriculturists to the capabilities of this long-neglected tract. He says: "On the wastes you find the heath growing knee-high, -a proof that the land has strength; you frequently find tall ferns mixing their bright green or yellow fans with these purple bushes; yet fern is an unfailing sign that the land has depth as well as goodness, and wherever ferns grow, unless indeed the elevation be too great, wheat might be reaped. The moors are divided into large sheep-walks for neighbouring farms. The sheep, a dwindled breed, are kept for their wool, and are sometimes left to die on the hills of old age, in the snow. The rent may be 1s. or perhaps 2s. an acre. Sometimes you find large pieces of the best land enclosed with a high fence, and you hope that the owner is about to begin tilling his freehold. On the contrary, the object of this improvement is to keep out the only sign of farming, the sheep, and to preserve the best of the land—because where the land is best the covert is highest—an undisturbed realm for the blackcock. Every blackcock killed by an owner of these moors has cost more, I am convinced, than a full-fed ox; though, indeed, it is nothing new that sporting should impede farming."

EXMOUTH, a chapelry in the p. of Littleham, Devon, 10 m. SE of Exeter, at the mouth of the Exe. Area 2,260 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,909; in 1841, 4,356. The town was originally but a small fishing-hamlet, but is now a well-frequented watering-place. Many good houses have been built for the accommodation of visitors, and there are hot and cold baths, and a good assembly room. It is well protected from the NE and SE winds by the hills behind it; the surrounding scenery is picturesque, and the air highly salubrious. In the reign of Edward this port furnished 10 vessels, and 193 men, to aid the king in his war with France. E. gives the title of Viscount to the Pellew family.

EXMOUTH GULF, a large inlet on the NW coast of Australia, 34 m. wide at its entrance be- EYB tween NW. Cape, in S lat. 21° 47′ 40″, E long. 114° 3' 20", and Cape Locker; and 45 m. deep. Its E side is low and lined with islands; under the W shore are some bays which afford anchorage on a rocky bottom.
EXMOUTH (Mount), a summit in Arbuthnot range, in Australia, in S lat. 31° 12', E long. 148° 141'. Alt. 3,000 ft.
EXNING, a parish of Suffolk, 2 m. NW of Newmarket. Area 5,710 acres. Pop. 1,259.
EXOUDON, a town of France, in the dep. of Deux Sevres, cant. of Saint-Heraye, situated on the Sevre-Niortaise, 7 m. SE of St. Maixent. Pop. 1,729.
EXPECTATION BAY, an anchorage in Concepcion strait, in Patagonia, in S lat. 50° 25', W long. 74° 13'.
EXPLOITS (BAY OF), an inlet on the E coast of Newfoundland, in N lat. 49° 45'.
